issues.body:
## Problem

Currently chat history is a JSON file in `.imp/chats/`, but artifacts (charts, tables, generated files) and tool outputs are scattered or lost. Deleting a chat doesn't clean up its artifacts. There's no way to go back to an old chart or table from a previous conversation.

## Solution

### Self-contained chat folders

Each chat gets its own subfolder:

```
.imp/chats/<chat-id>/
  chat.json          — conversation history (as today)
  artifacts/
    chart_1.png      — rendered charts
    table_1.json     — table data
    mermaid_1.png    — diagrams
    report.md        — any generated files
  tools/             — tool outputs specific to this chat
```

- **Delete chat = delete folder** — everything goes, nothing left behind
- Files stay as real files (not base64 in JSON) so they're browsable in a file manager
- Chat JSON references artifacts by relative path

### Loadable artifacts

Every table, chart, or diagram stored in a chat should be:

- **Loadable to dashboard** — click to open in the interactive dashboard view (when #135 lands)
- **Browsable across chats** — if an older chat has a useful table, you can find and load it
- **Downloadable as PNG** — for presentations, reports, sharing outside Imp

### What counts as an artifact

- Rendered charts (plotly, mermaid, gantt, kanban, etc.)
- Data tables (JSON, CSV)
- Generated files (README, scripts, configs)
- Any file the agent creates during the chat

## Implementation notes

- Migrate from `.imp/chats/<timestamp>_<id>.json` to `.imp/chats/<id>/chat.json`
- Agent's Write/Bash outputs that create files should be captured as artifacts
- Add download button on rendered charts/tables in chat UI
- Old chats without folders still load fine (backward compat)
issues.title: Self-contained chat folders: history, artifacts, and tools in one place

issues.body:
## Vision

Add an interactive dashboard layer to the chat window for live widgets (video feeds, click events, interactive charts, remote control). Unify renderers into the tools system. Make automations composable and saveable like Blender add-on presets.

## Features

### 1. Live widgets in chat
- **Video feed** — embed camera streams, get click events with coordinates (for robotics, #111)
- **Interactive graphs** — clickable charts that send events back to the agent
- **Remote control** — buttons, sliders, toggles that control external systems via chat
- **Custom HTML panels** — render arbitrary HTML widgets inline in chat messages

### 2. Unify renderers as tools
- Current renderers (mermaid, plotly, gantt, kanban, etc.) are a separate system
- Move them into the tools pattern — each renderer becomes a tool
- One system to maintain, one way to extend

### 3. Composable automation presets
Like Blender's add-on/preset system — not visual programming, just a systematic list-based approach:
- **Presets** — saved configurations of chats + workflows + tools that solve a specific problem
- **Save/load** — save a working automation as a preset, load it later or in another project
- **Import/export** — share presets between Imp instances (file-based, no server needed initially)
- **Browse** — list available presets, see what each one does, activate/deactivate
- Examples: "robotics game session", "excel table converter", "weekly triage", "PR review flow"

Each preset is just a folder with workflows, tools, and a config — no drag-and-drop, no graph UI, no visual wiring. You compose by combining existing pieces in a list.

### 4. [Later] Central repository
- Import presets from a shared server
- Browse community presets
- Publish your own

## Why

- Text-only chat limits what you can build (robotics needs video, data needs charts)
- Renderers and tools being separate is unnecessary complexity
- Presets make it easy to solve recurring problems without rebuilding from scratch
- Same pattern works for games, data processing, project management — any domain

## Depends on
- #111 (Robotics interface — first consumer of video feed + click events)
- #129 (Cached tool list — tools need to be discoverable)
issues.title: Interactive dashboard + composable automation presets

issues.body:
## Concept

Each chat can optionally be a "task" — a self-contained unit of work that creates a git branch, does the work, and finishes with either a merge or discard.

## Flow

### Start task (workflow or button)
1. Create a git branch from current state (e.g. `imp/<chat-title>`)
2. Open a new chat linked to that branch
3. Agent works on the branch — all changes are isolated

### Finish task (two outcomes)
- **Merge** — create a PR (or direct merge to main), clean up branch
- **Discard** — delete the branch, all changes gone, back to where you started

### Plain chat (no task)
Regular chats still work as before — no branch, no merge flow. Good for questions, read-only queries, status checks.

## Implementation ideas

- `workflows/start_task/` — built-in workflow that creates branch + opens chat
- `tools/imp/start_branch.py` — create branch from current HEAD
- `tools/imp/finish_task.py --merge` or `--discard` — end the task
- Chat UI could show a banner when on a task branch ("Working on: imp/fix-login — [Merge] [Discard]")
- The Foreman prompt should know when it's on a task branch

## What this enables

- Safe experimentation — every task is reversible
- Clean git history — each task is one branch, one PR
- Same pattern works for any management tool built as an Imp workflow
- Optional — not forced on every chat

## Open questions

- Should the agent auto-detect "this needs a branch" or always require explicit start?
- Naming convention for branches?
- Should discard require confirmation?
issues.title: Task workflow: branch-per-chat with merge or discard

issues.body:
## Problem

The Foreman agent currently executes Write, Python, and Bash commands without asking the user first. This is risky — a mistake can overwrite files or run destructive commands.

## Solution

Before executing these tools, the agent should pause and show the user what it's about to do, then wait for confirmation:

### Write
- Show a **diff** of the proposed changes (what will change in the file)
- User approves or rejects before the write happens

### Python / Bash
- Show the **command** that's about to run
- User approves or rejects before execution

### Read, Glob, Grep, etc.
- No confirmation needed — these are read-only and safe

## Implementation

Use the `can_use_tool` hook in the Foreman agent to intercept Write/Bash/Python tool calls. Send a WebSocket message asking the user to confirm, wait for their response, then allow or deny.
issues.title: Require user confirmation before Write, Python, and Bash execution

issues.body:
## Problem

The Foreman prompt currently auto-discovers tools on every request via `tools.build_tool_list_for_prompt()`. As tools and workflows stabilize, this is unnecessary overhead. The agent should load the list once at startup.

## Solution

- Load tool and workflow lists once when the server starts
- Cache them in memory
- Include a reload mechanism (e.g. `python tools/imp/reload_tools.py` or an API endpoint) for when tools/workflows change during a session
- The Foreman system prompt gets the cached list instead of scanning on every request

## Why

- Faster response time (no filesystem scan per request)
- Tools/workflows change rarely during a session
- Explicit reload gives the user control over when changes take effect
issues.title: Cache tool/workflow list at startup, add manual reload

issues.body:
## Problem

The Foreman agent jumps straight to raw Bash/Python when solving tasks. It doesn't check if there's already a workflow or tool that does what the user wants.

## Expected priority

When the agent needs to do something, it should check in this order:

1. **Workflows** — is there a workflow that does this (or close enough)?
2. **Tools** — is there a tool script that handles this?
3. **Python** — write/run a Python script
4. **Bash** — raw shell commands as last resort

## Why

- Workflows and tools are tested, documented, reusable
- Raw bash is fragile and hard to audit
- Users build up a library of workflows/tools over time — the agent should use them
- Connects to #118 (list_workflows and run_workflow tools for discovery)

## Implementation

- Update the Foreman system prompt to enforce this priority
- The agent should call `list_workflows` / check available tools before writing new code
- If a workflow is a close match, suggest running it
- If the difference is major, ask the user before creating something new
issues.title: Agent tool priority: workflows > tools > python > bash

issues.body:
## Problem

The setup wizard can only link to an existing GitHub repo. If someone copies Imp into a new/empty folder, there's no way to create a repo and push the contents from the browser.

## Solution

Add a `create_repo` tool to `tools/github/` that:

1. Runs `git init` if the folder isn't a git repo
2. Creates a GitHub repo via `gh repo create`
3. Adds the remote, commits all files, pushes

Then wire it into the setup agent's checklist — when `detect_repo_from_git` finds no repo, offer to create one (in addition to listing existing repos).

### Setup agent flow change

Current step 3:
> Call `detect_repo_from_git`. If no repo, list existing repos and ask admin to choose.

New step 3:
> Call `detect_repo_from_git`. If no repo, ask: create a new GitHub repo from this folder, or link to an existing one?

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `tools/github/create_repo.py` — git init, gh repo create, add remote, commit, push
- [ ] Setup agent offers "create new repo" when no git origin found
- [ ] Works from a completely empty (non-git) folder with files in it
issues.title: Setup wizard: create GitHub repo and push folder contents

issues.body:
## Problem

When P button opens a chat, the workflow/tool panel locks immediately and stays locked until the agent finishes. This means the user can't even browse the panel while typing their instructions.

## Fix

Only apply the lock when `isWorking` is true (agent is processing). When waiting for user input, the panel stays open for editing.
issues.title: Lock workflow/tool panel only while agent is working, not while chat is open

issues.body:
## Problem

The Foreman agent has no way to discover or run existing workflows. When a user asks to do something, the agent tries to solve it from scratch instead of checking if there's already a workflow that does what the user wants (or something close).

## Solution

Add two tools to `tools/imp/`:

### `list_workflows.py`
- Lists all workflows with their README content
- Agent can scan available workflows before starting work
- Output: workflow name, description, step count, README summary

### `run_workflow.py`
- Runs a workflow by name
- Agent can execute an existing workflow instead of building from scratch

### Agent behavior change

Update the Foreman system prompt so the agent:

1. **Before solving a problem**, checks `list_workflows` to see if an existing workflow already does what the user wants
2. If a workflow is a **close match**, suggests running it
3. If the difference is **major**, asks the user: "Would you like me to create a new workflow/tools based on the existing ones, or modify the existing workflow?"
4. Only builds from scratch when nothing relevant exists

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `tools/imp/list_workflows.py` — lists workflows with READMEs
- [ ] `tools/imp/run_workflow.py` — runs a workflow by name
- [ ] Agent checks existing workflows before solving from scratch
- [ ] Agent asks user when the match is partial (major differences)
issues.title: Agent tools: list_workflows and run_workflow for workflow discovery

## Problem

The P (AI edit) button on workflows opens a chat with an empty text box and no guidance. The agent doesn't know what the user likely wants, and on mobile it's hard to type a prompt from scratch.

## Solution

- Add one line to the instructions sent to the agent: ask the user if they want to adjust the steps to match the README, or something else entirely
- Chat title should be "Edit: <workflow name>" instead of "Edit: README.md"
- Text box stays clean (removed the code that pre-filled it with the full instructions string)

issues.body:
## Bug

Agent responses are rendered twice in the chat UI. The entire response (tables, text, mermaid diagrams) appears duplicated.

## Root cause

In `imp-chat.js`, the `done` WebSocket handler appends `msg.full_text` to `agentText` when it shouldn't:

```javascript
if (!agentText.includes(msg.full_text) && msg.full_text) {
    agentText += msg.full_text;
}
```

`agentText` accumulates streamed tokens **plus** injected HTML blocks (tool `<details>`, thinking `<details>`). The `includes()` check fails because `full_text` (plain text) isn't a substring of `agentText` (which has interleaved HTML). So the full response gets appended again, duplicating everything.

## Fix

`full_text` is a fallback for when streaming didn't deliver the text. If `agentText` already has streamed content, don't append.
issues.title: Chat response rendered twice (duplicate content on done)

issues.body:
## Summary

Add a game mode on top of the robotics interface (#111) where students score points by picking up QR-coded blocks in specific patterns.

## Concept

- Blocks on the workbench each have a **QR code** (identified by the existing camera + OpenCV tracking)
- Students must pick up blocks in a **specific pattern** to score points
- **Tetris-like mechanic**: after completing a pattern, the next pattern is revealed
- More complex patterns = more points
- Visual feedback on the video feed showing which blocks to pick next

## Depends on

- #111 (Robotics teaching interface) — needs click-to-move, QR detection, and calibration working first

## Compatibility considerations

- QR detection must handle **multiple QR codes** simultaneously (blocks + robot head)
- Each block QR encodes a unique ID
- Game state tracking: which blocks have been picked, current pattern, score
- Pattern display overlay on the video feed
- Leaderboard / score display in the UI

## Open questions

- How are patterns defined? (config file, random generation, difficulty levels?)
- Should there be a time limit per pattern?
- Team mode vs individual?
issues.title: Game mode: Tetris-like block pickup patterns with scoring

issues.body:
## Summary

Add a Robot tab to the Imp UI for teaching 7-9th graders robotics with a Dobot MG400.

## Setup

- **Robot**: Dobot MG400 (TCP control — dashboard :29999, movement :30003)
- **Camera**: IP camera, top-down view of workbench
- **Tracking**: QR code on robot head, detected via OpenCV

## Features

### Split-screen UI (Robot tab)
- Left panel: live video feed from IP camera (MJPEG stream)
- Right panel: chat interface for AI-assisted robot control

### Click-to-move
- Student clicks on video feed → pixel coordinates mapped to workbench coordinates via calibration → robot moves there

### Pre-built action buttons
- Go Down, Activate Pump, Pickup, Bring to Dispenser, Let Go
- Available both as UI buttons and as chat tools (AI can call them too)

### Calibration system
- Affine transform mapping pixel coords ↔ robot workbench coords
- Move robot to 3+ known positions, detect QR code pixel position, compute transform matrix
- Eliminates drift between click position and where robot head actually goes
- Persisted to `.imp/robot_calibration.json`

### QR position tracking
- OpenCV `QRCodeDetector` on camera frames
- Crosshair overlay on detected position
- Real-time position feedback

## Architecture

```
server/robot.py       — RobotConnection, VideoStream, Calibration classes
static/imp-robot.js   — Robot tab UI, click handler, calibration wizard
tools/robot/          — Tool scripts (connect, move, actions, status, calibrate)
```

New API endpoints under `/api/robot/*` in `render_route.py`.

## Dependencies

- `opencv-python-headless>=4.8`
- `numpy>=1.24`

## Notes

Full implementation plan saved at `.claude/plans/jiggly-floating-milner.md`.
issues.title: Robotics teaching interface: MG400 + camera + calibration

issues.body:
## Vision

The **P button** everywhere in the UI should open a **new chat session** pre-loaded with the relevant files and instructions. The user describes what they want in natural language, the AI reads the files, makes changes, and the user can iterate through conversation. When done, the files are updated.

This replaces the current "single-shot LLM call" pattern with a proper interactive session where the user can course-correct.

## How it works

### P button flow

1. User clicks **P** on any item (tool, tool group, workflow, workflow step)
2. System gathers the **context files** relevant to that item
3. Opens a **new chat** in the Chat tab with:
   - A system message listing the files and their current content
   - Instructions for the AI (what kind of item it is, what can be changed)
   - The user's prompt from the textarea (if they typed one)
4. User chats with the AI — asks for changes, reviews, iterates
5. AI edits the files through the chat (using the existing foreman agent)
6. User switches back to Tools/Workflows tab — changes are reflected

### Context per item type

| Item | Files loaded into chat | Instructions |
|------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Tool script** | `tools/{group}/{name}.py`, `tools/{group}/{name}.step.py` | "Edit this tool's code and/or step template based on the user's request" |
| **Tool group** | `tools/{group}/README.md`, list of all scripts in group | "Edit the group README or create/modify tools in this group" |
| **Workflow** | `workflows/{name}/README.md`, all `step_*.py` files | "Edit workflow steps, add/remove steps, update README" |
| **Workflow step** | `workflows/{name}/{step}.py`, the README for context | "Edit this specific step's code" |

### Python interface

A `ChatContext` object that any UI element can use to open a pre-loaded chat:

```python
class ChatContext:
    """Defines what files and instructions to load into a new chat."""
    files: list[str]           # paths relative to project root
    instructions: str          # system-level instructions for the AI
    user_prompt: str           # initial user message (from textarea)
    
    def to_system_message(self) -> str:
        """Build the system message with file contents and instructions."""
        ...
```

### API

```
POST /api/chat/new-with-context
{
    "files": ["tools/github/list_issues.py", "tools/github/list_issues.step.py"],
    "instructions": "Edit this tool based on the user's request.",
    "user_prompt": "Add a --sort flag that sorts by creation date"
}
→ {"chat_id": "abc123", "redirect": "/chat/abc123"}
```

The endpoint:
1. Creates a new chat session
2. Loads file contents into the initial context
3. Sends the user's prompt as the first message
4. Returns the chat ID so the UI can switch to the Chat tab

### UI changes

- P button textarea gets a **"Open in Chat"** submit button (instead of "Run AI")
- Clicking it switches to the Chat tab with the new session active
- The chat has all relevant files pre-loaded
- User can iterate, ask follow-ups, review changes
- Files are edited in-place by the foreman agent (existing tool use)

## Why this is better

- **Current**: P sends a single prompt → LLM returns code → hope it's right
- **New**: P opens a conversation → user can review, ask questions, iterate → code converges to what they actually want
- Same AI, same tools, but the user stays in the loop

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `ChatContext` Python class for building pre-loaded chat sessions
- [ ] `POST /api/chat/new-with-context` endpoint
- [ ] P button on tools opens chat with tool files + instructions
- [ ] P button on tool groups opens chat with group README + tool list
- [ ] P button on workflows opens chat with all step files + README
- [ ] P button on workflow steps opens chat with that step file
- [ ] Chat tab activates with the new session
- [ ] AI can read and edit the pre-loaded files through conversation
- [ ] User can iterate and course-correct before changes are final
issues.title: [P9] P button opens chat with context — AI edits files through conversation

issues.body:
## Problem

No way to test tools/steps in isolation. You have to run the entire workflow.

## Solution

Add a **Testing** section to each tool in the Tools tab, below the existing Script and Workflow Template sections. Three parts:

### Context In (editable textarea)
- JSON input that gets passed to `run(context)` 
- Pre-filled from last run's context or empty `{}`
- User can edit to test different inputs

### Context Out (editable textarea)
- Shows the result JSON after running
- Editable so user can manually adjust before using as input to the next step

### Log
- Shows stdout/stderr and execution details from the test run
- Duration, ok/error status

### Run button
- Runs the `.step.py` template's `run(context)` with the Context In content
- Fills Context Out with the result
- Fills Log with execution details

## API

```
POST /api/tool-debug
{
    "group": "github",
    "name": "list_issues",
    "context": {"previous_results": []}
}
→ {"ok": true, "output": "...", "duration_s": 1.23, "result": {...}}
```

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Testing section with Context In, Context Out, Log
- [ ] Run button executes step template with Context In
- [ ] POST /api/tool-debug endpoint
- [ ] Both textareas are editable
issues.title: [P5.3] Debug panel: run tool/step with editable context from last run

issues.body:
## Problem

The Workflows tab and Tools tab share nearly identical UI patterns:
- Collapsible cards with summary bars (name, meta, action buttons)
- Edit/Rename/Copy/Delete buttons on the header
- Expandable child items (steps / tool scripts) with their own buttons
- Edit mode that swaps buttons and shows textareas
- LLM spinner states (configuring, describing)
- Open/closed state preservation across re-renders
- Lazy-loading of detail content on toggle

Both are implemented as separate render functions with duplicated HTML generation, CSS classes, state variables, and event handling. Fixing a bug in one (e.g. open state lost, buttons not hiding) requires fixing the same thing in the other.

## Solution

Extract a shared **list component** — a JS function/object that handles:

1. **Rendering** — collapsible parent cards with child items, action buttons
2. **State** — open/closed tracking, edit mode per item, spinner states
3. **CRUD buttons** — Edit/Copy/Rename/Delete with consistent behavior
4. **LLM integration** — spinner overlay, P button prompt flow
5. **Lazy loading** — detail content loaded on first toggle

Each tab configures the component with:
- Data source (API endpoint)
- Parent/child field mapping (group→tools, workflow→steps)
- Available actions per item type
- Custom content renderers (tool source code, workflow step output)

## Scope

- Extract common rendering + state logic into a reusable JS object
- Workflows tab and Tools tab both use it
- Queue tab stays separate (different UX pattern)
- Keep it in chat.html — no build step, no modules, just a shared object

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Shared list component handles: render, open state, edit mode, spinners
- [ ] Workflows tab uses the component
- [ ] Tools tab uses the component
- [ ] Bug fixes apply to both tabs automatically
- [ ] No visual regression in either tab
issues.title: [P8] Unify Workflow and Tools UI into shared list component

issues.body:
## Problem

When clicking Start on a workflow, the button stays active until the first server response comes back. If the first step takes a long time (e.g. 30s server start), the user can click Start multiple times or thinks nothing happened.

## Expected

Start button disables and shows spinner **immediately on click**, before the fetch to `/start` even fires. This was partially implemented but the `loadWorkflows()` re-render can reset the button state.

## Fix

The instant feedback code in `startWorkflow()` already sets `btn.disabled = true` and swaps the status icon, but needs to be bulletproof against re-renders during the waiting period.
issues.title: [P4.24] Workflow Start button should disable immediately on click

issues.body:
## Vision

A developer runs a single Python script locally and gets **live bidirectional file sync** with the Imp server. Edit a tool in VS Code locally → it appears on the server instantly. Server-side LLM updates a file → it syncs to your local folder. Kill the script → sync stops.

## How it works

### Server side

A workflow with two steps:

1. **Start sync server** — opens an API endpoint for file sync (SSE or polling)
   - `GET /api/sync/manifest` — returns all files with hashes/mtimes
   - `GET /api/sync/file?path=tools/github/push.py` — download a file
   - `POST /api/sync/file` — upload a changed file
   - Watches `tools/`, `workflows/`, `renderers/`, `public/` for changes
2. **Pause** — pushes to queue with:
   - Status: "Developer sync active"
   - **Download link** in the queue popup: `http://<server-ip>:8421/imp-sync.py` — the pre-configured sync script ready to run
   - Action: "Stop sync" → shuts down the sync endpoint
   - Later: timer-based auto-stop (e.g. 4 hours)

The queue popup is how the developer gets the script — start the sync workflow, open the queue item, click the download link, drop it in a folder, run it.

### Client side

A single `imp-sync.py` script that:

1. User downloads it from the **queue popup** (link with server IP already baked in)
2. Drops it in any folder
3. Runs: `python imp-sync.py`
4. On first run: pulls all files into local folder structure (`tools/`, `workflows/`, etc.)
5. Watches local files for changes (mtime-based, no dependencies beyond stdlib)
6. Polls server for remote changes every 2-3s
7. Bidirectional sync: last-write-wins based on mtime
8. Ctrl+C → clean exit, no residue

### Sync scope

| Folder | Synced |
|--------|--------|
| `tools/` | Yes — all `.py`, `.step.py`, `.md` files |
| `workflows/` | Yes — step scripts, README.md (NOT `last_run.json`, `__pycache__`) |
| `renderers/` | Yes |
| `public/` | Yes |

### Zero-dependency client

The sync script uses only Python stdlib:
- `urllib.request` for HTTP
- `json` for manifest parsing
- `hashlib` for change detection
- `os` / `time` for file watching
- `pathlib` for path handling

No pip install, no virtualenv, no setup. Just `python imp-sync.py`.

## Conflict resolution

- **Default**: last-write-wins (mtime comparison)
- If both sides changed the same file since last sync: keep both as `file.py` and `file.py.conflict`, print a warning
- Deletions sync: if server deletes a file, local copy is removed (and vice versa)

## Server API endpoints

```
GET  /api/sync/manifest          → {"files": {"tools/github/push.py": {"hash": "abc", "mtime": 1234}, ...}}
GET  /api/sync/file?path=...     → file content (raw)
POST /api/sync/file              → {"path": "tools/github/push.py", "content": "...", "mtime": 1234}
DELETE /api/sync/file?path=...   → delete file on server
GET  /imp-sync.py                → generated sync script with server URL baked in
```

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Sync workflow: start endpoint + pause in queue
- [ ] Queue popup shows download link for `imp-sync.py` with server IP baked in
- [ ] Server API: manifest, download, upload, delete
- [ ] `imp-sync.py`: zero-dependency, pre-configured download
- [ ] Bidirectional sync with mtime-based conflict detection
- [ ] Syncs `tools/`, `workflows/`, `renderers/`, `public/`
- [ ] Excludes `__pycache__`, `last_run.json`, `.pyc`
- [ ] Clean shutdown on Ctrl+C
- [ ] Queue-based stop: user clicks OK in queue to end sync session
issues.title: [P7] Developer sync: live file sync between server and local editor

**Scope**

Split the Workflows tab into two panels: workflow editor on the left,
tool browser on the right. Drag a tool from the browser → adds a step
to the workflow.

## Layout

\`\`\`
[Queue] [Chat] [Workflows]
─────────────────────────────────────────────────
│                        │                       │
│  Workflow Editor       │  Tool Browser         │
│                        │                       │
│  ▼ weekly_triage       │  ▼ github/            │
│    README...           │    • list_issues      │
│                        │    • list_prs          │
│    ✅ Sync issues 1.0s │    • open_issue        │
│    ✅ Heuristics  0.0s │    • close_issue       │
│    ⏸ Review chart      │    • moderate_issues   │
│    ⏳ Post summary     │    • solve_issues      │
│                        │    • ...               │
│    [+ Add step]        │                       │
│                        │  ▼ heuristics/         │
│                        │    • calibrate         │
│                        │                       │
│                        │  ▼ pipeline/           │
│                        │    • sync_issues       │
│                        │    • render_chart      │
│                        │                       │
│                        │  [Search tools...]     │
│                        │                       │
─────────────────────────────────────────────────
\`\`\`

## Tool browser (right panel)

- Lists all tools from \`tools/\` folder (auto-discovered)
- Grouped by folder (github/, heuristics/, pipeline/)
- Each tool shows:
  - Name
  - First line of docstring (description)
  - Click → shows full details in a popup (args, README)
- **Click a tool → adds it as a new step** to the selected workflow
  - Creates a step .py with the tool call pre-filled
  - Docstring set to the tool's description
  - User can [edit] to customize

## Workflow editor (left panel)

- Same as current workflow display but editable:
  - Reorder steps (drag or up/down buttons)
  - Delete steps
  - [edit] to change docstring (triggers AI code update later, #91)
  - [+ Add step] button at the bottom

## Future: central repository search

Once the Nexus server exists (#70), the tool browser gets a search
bar that queries the central repository. Found tools can be installed
locally and used in workflows. For now, only local \`tools/\` folder.

## Implementation

- \`chat.html\` — split workflows tab into two-panel layout
- \`server/render_route.py\` — \`/api/tools\` endpoint listing all tools
  with descriptions (uses \`tools.discover()\` + \`tools.list_executables()\`)
- \`workflows/__init__.py\` — add_step(), remove_step(), reorder_steps()

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Workflows tab has two panels (left: editor, right: browser)
- [ ] Tool browser lists all tools grouped by folder
- [ ] Clicking a tool adds a step to the selected workflow
- [ ] Steps can be reordered and deleted
- [ ] [+ Add step] button at bottom of workflow
- [ ] Tool details popup (description, args, README)
- [ ] Layout responsive (stacks vertically on mobile)

issues.body:
## Scope

A **Tools tab** in the UI for browsing, editing, and debugging tool scripts.
No Python knowledge needed — edit the description, LLM updates the code.

## What it looks like

### Tool browser (left panel)
- Tools grouped by folder: `github/` → push, pull, list_issues, etc.
- Each tool shows one-line description (from docstring)
- Click a tool → detail view opens on right

### Detail view (right panel)
- **Summary**: human-readable docstring rendered as markdown
- **Code toggle**: show/hide actual Python source (for debugging)
- **Edit**: change the description → LLM updates the Python to match
- **Debug panel**: set arguments, run the tool, see output

### LLM edit flow
1. Click Edit → description becomes editable textarea
2. User changes text (e.g. "add a --label filter" or "change default limit to 50")
3. Click Save → spinner → LLM gets old description + code + new description
4. LLM updates Python → file saved → view refreshes

### Debug/run panel
- Auto-discovers argparse arguments via AST
- Renders input fields per argument (dropdowns for choices, checkboxes for booleans)
- Run button → subprocess execution → show stdout/stderr
- 30s timeout

## API endpoints
- `GET /api/tools` — full tool listing with descriptions + args
- `GET /api/tool-detail?group=X&name=Y` — source, docstring, parsed args
- `POST /api/tool-edit` — LLM-powered code update
- `POST /api/tool-run` — debug execution with user-provided args

## Files
- `chat.html` — new Tools tab, JS functions
- `server/render_route.py` — new endpoints + AST arg extraction

**Scope** Workflows follow the same pattern as tools and renderers: each workflow is a folder with a README and step scripts. A trigger page lets you start workflows and monitor progress. Steps can pause for human input via the queue. ## Workflow folder structure Same pattern as \`tools//\` and \`renderers//\`: \`\`\` workflows/ ├── __init__.py # discovery + runner │ ├── weekly_triage/ # one workflow │ ├── README.md # what it does, when to run │ ├── step_1_sync.py # step scripts run in order │ ├── step_2_heuristics.py │ ├── step_3_review.py # can pause() for human │ └── step_4_report.py │ ├── deploy_release/ │ ├── README.md │ ├── step_1_changelog.py │ ├── step_2_approve.py # pause() │ └── step_3_publish.py │ └── daily_standup/ ├── README.md └── step_1_summary.py \`\`\` Each step script is a regular Python file with a \`run()\` function: \`\`\`python # workflows/weekly_triage/step_1_sync.py \"\"\"Sync issues from GitHub.\"\"\" import subprocess def run(context): result = subprocess.run( ["python", "pipeline/sync_issues.py"], capture_output=True, text=True, ) return {"ok": result.returncode == 0, "output": result.stdout} \`\`\` For human pause points: \`\`\`python # workflows/weekly_triage/step_3_review.py \"\"\"Review the triage results before applying.\"\"\" def run(context): return { "pause": True, "title": "Review triage results", "detail_html": "

Check the burndown chart...

", "actions": [ {"label": "Approve", "action": "approve"}, {"label": "Skip", "action": "skip"}, ], } \`\`\` ## Trigger page (3rd tab) \`\`\` [Queue] [Chat] [Workflows] ───────────────────────────── \`\`\` ### /workflows tab shows: - List of discovered workflows (from \`workflows/*/\`) - Each shows: name, README first line, step count - **Start** button per workflow - Status: idle / running (step 2/4) / paused / done / error - Optional **API** toggle → shows \`POST /api/workflows//start\` ### Running workflow shows: \`\`\` ▼ weekly_triage (running — step 2/4) ✅ step_1_sync · 0.5s 🔄 step_2_heuristics · running... ⏳ step_3_review ⏳ step_4_report \`\`\` ## How steps connect to queue When a step returns \`{"pause": True, ...}\`, the runner: 1. Pushes an item to the queue (via \`server/work_queue.py\`) 2. Pauses the workflow 3. When the human resolves the queue item, the workflow resumes 4. The resolved action is passed to the next step via \`context\` ## Implementation ### New files - \`workflows/__init__.py\` — discover + WorkflowRunner class - \`workflows//\` — workflow folders with step scripts + README - \`chat.html\` — add Workflows tab ### API \`\`\` GET /api/workflows → list discovered workflows POST /api/workflows//start → start a workflow GET /api/workflows/ → status + step progress POST /api/workflows//abort → cancel running workflow \`\`\` ### WorkflowRunner (~80 lines) \`\`\`python class WorkflowRunner: def __init__(self, name, steps): self.steps = steps self.current = 0 self.status = "idle" self.results = [] async def run(self): for step in self.steps: self.status = "running" result = step.run(self.context) if result.get("pause"): self.status = "paused" # push to queue, wait for resolution self.results.append(result) self.status = "done" \`\`\` ## What stays unchanged - \`tools/\` — workflows call tools via subprocess - \`renderers/\` — workflows can render charts - \`server/work_queue.py\` — queue receives pause items - \`server/guard.py\` — commands run through security ## Acceptance criteria - [ ] \`workflows/__init__.py\` with discovery + runner - [ ] Step scripts with \`run(context)\` contract - [ ] Pause steps push to queue, resume on resolution - [ ] Workflows tab in chat.html - [ ] Start/abort/status API endpoints - [ ] Step progress visible (✅/🔄/⏳ per step) - [ ] At least one example workflow (weekly_triage)

issues.body:
## Problem

The `TurnUI` class in `server/turn_ui.py` defines a callback interface with methods like `show_plan`, `tool_started`, `stream_token`, etc. — but the docstrings don't explain **when** each method fires or in what order. The class docstring just says "Callback interface for structured tool-call rendering", which undersells what it actually does.

A reader has to trace through `foreman_agent.py` and `ToolTracker` to understand the lifecycle.

## Proposal

Add docstrings that explain:

1. **Per-method**: when each callback fires (e.g. "once per tool, right before execution") and what the arguments represent.
2. **Class-level on `TurnUI`**: the typical call sequence in a single turn, e.g.:
   ```
   thinking_update → show_plan → tool_started → tool_finished → ... → stream_token → ... → stream_end
   ```
3. **Class-level on `ToolTracker`**: its role as the bridge between the agent dispatch loop and the UI — it batches tool-use blocks into `PlanItem`s and fires start/finish callbacks.

## Why

- The interface looks like an abstract base class but is actually a **concrete per-event contract** with a fixed lifecycle. Making that explicit reduces onboarding friction.
- There's currently one producer (`foreman_agent.py`) and one real consumer (`WebSocketTurnUI` in `chat_ws.py`). Documenting the contract makes it safer to add new consumers or events.
issues.title: Improve turn_ui.py docstrings to explain callback lifecycle

issues.body:
**Scope**

The entire MCP server layer is unnecessary. Claude Code already has
native Bash, Read, Write tools. We're wrapping \`gh issue list\` in a
Python function → MCP tool → so Claude can call... \`gh issue list\`.

Delete the MCP layer entirely. Use Claude Code's native tools with a
\`can_use_tool\` hook that routes through guard + intercept for security.

## Current state (1,674 lines of unnecessary wrapping)

\`\`\`
Claude wants to list issues
  → calls MCP tool "list_issues"
    → MCP handler calls do_list_issues()
      → which calls intercept.execute_command(["gh", "issue", "list"])
        → intercept classifies, guards, budgets, runs subprocess
\`\`\`

30 do_* functions, 30 @tool registrations, 400-line MCP factory —
all just to run shell commands that Claude Code can run natively.

## Target state (~100 lines)

\`\`\`
Claude wants to list issues
  → uses native Bash tool: gh issue list
    → can_use_tool hook intercepts
      → intercept.py classifies (whitelist)
      → guard.py approves/denies (LLM check for writes)
      → budgets.py tracks spend
      → command runs (or is rejected)
\`\`\`

No MCP server. No do_* functions. No tool registration. The agent
is just:

\`\`\`python
async def dispatch(user_text, *, say, history, turn_ui):
    prompt = load_system_prompt()
    options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
        system_prompt=prompt,
        can_use_tool=security_hook,  # guard + intercept + budgets
        max_turns=20,
    )
    # ... SDK streaming loop (unchanged)
\`\`\`

## Security hook

\`\`\`python
async def security_hook(tool_name, tool_input, context):
    \"\"\"Called by Claude SDK before every tool use.\"\"\"
    if tool_name == "Bash":
        argv = parse_bash_command(tool_input["command"])
        classification = intercept.classify_command(argv)
        if classification.kind == "unknown":
            return Deny("Command not recognized")
        if classification.kind == "write":
            ok, reason = await guard.check_action(
                user_intent=context.user_message,
                proposed_command=tool_input["command"],
            )
            if not ok:
                return Deny(reason)
            if budgets.get_budgets().any_exhausted():
                return Deny("Budget exhausted")
    return Allow()
\`\`\`

## What gets deleted

| Section | Lines | Action |
|---------|-------|--------|
| 30 \`do_*\` functions | ~500 | **Delete** |
| \`_build_mcp_server()\` | ~400 | **Delete** |
| 30 \`@tool\` registrations | ~400 | **Delete** |
| \`_DISALLOWED_TOOLS\` | ~15 | **Delete** |
| \`allowed_tool_names\` list | ~30 | **Delete** |
| \`_shell_result()\` helper | ~20 | **Delete** |

**Total: ~1,365 lines deleted from foreman_agent.py**

## What stays

| Section | Lines | Why |
|---------|-------|-----|
| \`dispatch()\` | ~100 | SDK streaming loop |
| \`_load_system_prompt()\` | ~5 | Load prompt from file |
| \`security_hook()\` | ~30 | can_use_tool callback |
| Type aliases + imports | ~20 | SayFn, AskFn, etc. |

**foreman_agent.py: 1,674 → ~155 lines**

## System prompt

The \`tools/\` folder is irrelevant for the agent now — Claude Code
has its own tools. The system prompt just needs to say:

> You have access to Bash, Read, Write, etc. Use \`gh\` CLI for GitHub
> operations. Every command goes through a security hook that enforces
> budgets and requires guard approval for writes.

The prompt no longer needs to list 30 tool names.

## What happens to tools/ folder

It stays for the CRUD-managed executables (prompt configs for the
AI workflows like moderate_issues, solve_issues). But the agent
doesn't need to know about them — Claude just runs
\`python tools/github/moderate_issues.py --issue 42\` via Bash.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] MCP server deleted (\`_build_mcp_server\`, all \`@tool\` registrations)
- [ ] All 30 \`do_*\` functions deleted
- [ ] \`can_use_tool\` hook routes through guard + intercept + budgets
- [ ] Claude uses native Bash/Read/Write tools
- [ ] foreman_agent.py under 200 lines
- [ ] Security unchanged (same guard + intercept pipeline, different entry point)
- [ ] All tests updated
issues.title: [P4.32] thin foreman agent: load tools from tools/ folder, security in guard only

issues.body:
**Scope**

Chat history currently saves only the final answer text. This issue
makes it save the **full turn log** — thinking blocks, tool calls with
input/output, rendered artifacts — and ties temporary files to the
conversation lifecycle.

## What to save per turn

Each assistant turn should store not just the reply text but the full
structured log:

\`\`\`json
{
  "role": "assistant",
  "content": "Here are the open issues...",
  "thinking": ["The user wants...", "Let me call list_issues..."],
  "tool_calls": [
    {
      "name": "list_issues",
      "args": {"state": "open"},
      "status": "ok",
      "duration_s": 0.5,
      "output": "#42 OPEN..."
    }
  ],
  "artifacts": [
    {"type": "chart", "template": "gantt", "html_path": "gantt.html"}
  ],
  "timestamp": "2026-04-19T10:00:00Z"
}
\`\`\`

The WebSocket \`TurnUI\` already receives all this data — it just needs
to accumulate it into the turn record before saving.

## Per-conversation asset folder

\`\`\`
.imp/chats/
├── <id>.json                    # chat session (turns with full log)
└── <id>/                        # assets for this conversation
    ├── gantt.html               # rendered chart HTML
    ├── mermaid_abc123.png       # screenshot
    └── tool_0_list_issues.txt   # tool output log
\`\`\`

- Renderers and tool logs write to the session's folder
- \`chat_history.delete_session()\` removes both JSON + folder
- Viewer links (\`/public/chats/<id>/gantt.html\`) serve from session folder

## Orphan purge

\`chat_history.purge_orphans()\` scans \`.imp/chats/\` for:
- Asset folders with no matching JSON file → delete
- JSON files referencing assets that don't exist → clean references

Called on server startup.

## Historic chat view (read-only)

When loading a past chat from the sidebar:
- **No text input box** — the conversation is read-only
- **No status bar** — it's a log, not a live session
- **Date shown** under the chat title in small text:
  \`\`\`
  Issue triage burndown
  Apr 18, 2026 · 14:32
  \`\`\`
- All thinking blocks, tool calls, and artifacts render from the
  saved data (not re-fetched)
- Click "New" to start a fresh conversation

## Files touched

- \`server/chat_history.py\` — extend Turn dataclass with thinking/tools/artifacts,
  add \`purge_orphans()\`, session asset folder helpers
- \`server/chat_ws.py\` — accumulate full turn log in WebSocketTurnUI,
  save to session asset folder
- \`chat.html\` — read-only mode for historic chats (hide input/status),
  render thinking/tool blocks from saved data, show date under title
- \`server/render_route.py\` — serve session asset folders

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Assistant turns save thinking, tool calls (with output), and artifact refs
- [ ] Per-conversation asset folder created alongside JSON
- [ ] \`delete_session()\` removes both JSON and asset folder
- [ ] \`purge_orphans()\` cleans up orphaned folders/references
- [ ] Historic chats render as read-only (no input, no status bar)
- [ ] Date visible under chat title in the main area
- [ ] Tool blocks in historic view are expandable (same \`<details>\` UI)
- [ ] Thinking blocks in historic view are expandable
issues.title: [P4.31] chat history: full turn log with thinking/tools/artifacts, read-only historic view

issues.body: **Scope** Replace the 2,438-line Chainlit main.py with a single HTML file + WebSocket endpoint on the existing render server. No framework, no auth, no npm. The entire chat UI is ~300 lines of HTML/CSS/JS served as a static file. ## Why Chainlit fights us at every turn: - Auth middleware blocks the render server routes - \`cl.Step\` / \`cl.Message\` / \`cl.Image\` rendering model doesn't support status bars, fixed-position indicators, or reliable inline image updates - 2,438 lines of Python just to wire Chainlit primitives - Every UX improvement requires fighting the framework ## What we need ### 1. Chat sidebar (left panel) - List of saved conversations (from \`.imp/chats/*.json\`) - Click to load/resume - New chat button - Delete chat button - Shows \`[date time] title\` format (already implemented in chat_history) ### 2. Chat area (center) - Renders markdown (\`.md\` formatting — headers, bold, code blocks, links) - Inline images (\`\` tags pointing at render server URLs) - Mermaid/chart code blocks auto-rendered via render server (replace code block with \`\`) - User messages right-aligned, agent messages left-aligned - Streaming: tokens appear as they arrive via WebSocket ### 3. Chat input + status area (bottom) - Text input with send button - **Status line** below the input showing what the agent is doing: - \`Thinking...\` — waiting for first SDK response - \`Running list_issues()...\` — tool call in progress - \`Rendering chart...\` — screenshot in progress - Empty when idle (submit button visible) - Stop button when agent is working - Status is ALWAYS visible (fixed position, never scrolls away) ### 4. No auth - Single-admin local tool — no login, no password, no cookies - The render server already runs without auth on port 8421 - Chat endpoint joins the same server ## Architecture \`\`\` render server (port 8421, already exists) ├── GET /render/ → PNG/HTML (already works) ├── GET /health → status (already works) ├── GET / → serves chat.html (NEW) ├── WS /ws/chat → WebSocket for chat (NEW) ├── GET /api/chats → list conversations (NEW) ├── POST /api/chats → create new chat (NEW) ├── GET /api/chats/ → load conversation (NEW) └── DELETE /api/chats/ → delete conversation (NEW) \`\`\` ### WebSocket protocol Client → Server: \`\`\`json {"type": "message", "text": "show me the burndown chart"} {"type": "stop"} \`\`\` Server → Client: \`\`\`json {"type": "token", "text": "Here's the"} {"type": "status", "text": "Running list_issues()..."} {"type": "status", "text": ""} {"type": "tool_plan", "items": [{"name": "list_issues", "status": "running"}]} {"type": "image", "url": "/render/burndown", "alt": "Burndown chart"} {"type": "done", "full_text": "Here's the burndown..."} \`\`\` ### Markdown rendering Use a lightweight JS markdown library (marked.js — 8KB gzipped, CDN-loaded like mermaid.js in the renderer templates). Code blocks with \`\`\`mermaid\`\`\` are replaced with \`\` tags pointing at the render server. ## Files - \`chat.html\` — **new** — single HTML file (~300 lines) with embedded CSS + JS. Served by render server at \`/\`. - \`server/render_route.py\` — add WebSocket endpoint + REST API for chat CRUD + serve \`chat.html\` - \`server/chat_ws.py\` — **new** — WebSocket handler that calls \`foreman_agent.dispatch()\` and streams results back - \`main.py\` — **delete or gut** — Chainlit app no longer needed ## Migration 1. Build chat.html + WebSocket endpoint 2. Wire to existing \`foreman_agent.dispatch()\` 3. Test side-by-side with Chainlit (both can run on different ports) 4. Once working, delete main.py's Chainlit code ## What stays unchanged - \`server/foreman_agent.py\` — dispatch loop (already UI-agnostic) - \`server/turn_ui.py\` — TurnUI ABC (WebSocket implements it) - \`server/guard.py\` / \`server/intercept.py\` — guard + budgets - \`server/chat_history.py\` — persistence - \`tools/\` — all tool executables - \`renderers/\` — all renderer plugins - \`server/screenshot.py\` — html2image ## Acceptance criteria - [ ] Chat UI loads at \`http://127.0.0.1:8421/\` - [ ] Sidebar lists saved chats, click to load, new/delete buttons - [ ] Markdown renders correctly (headers, bold, code, links) - [ ] Mermaid code blocks render as inline images via render server - [ ] Streaming: tokens appear character-by-character via WebSocket - [ ] Status line below input always shows current agent activity - [ ] Status line NEVER scrolls away (fixed position) - [ ] Stop button cancels in-progress agent work - [ ] No auth — just open the URL and start chatting - [ ] Total new code under 500 lines (HTML + Python) - [ ] Chainlit (main.py) can be removed after migration

issues.body:
**Scope**

Emergency cleanup. Logic is duplicated across 3-4 files. The foreman
agent has 30 hardcoded functions that should be generic tool calls.
\`dispatcher.py\` is dead code (replaced by foreman_agent). The system
prompt is a 106-line inline string.

## Duplicated logic found

| Logic | Files | Copies | Fix |
|-------|-------|--------|-----|
| Config load/save | foreman_agent, main.py, setup_agent | **3x** | \`server/config.py\` |
| Repo detection from git | main.py, setup_agent | **2x** | \`server/config.py\` |
| LLM backend call | foreman_agent, dispatcher, guard, chat_history | **4x** | Single SDK helper |
| gh CLI wrappers | foreman_agent (14 funcs), setup_agent, main.py | **3x** | \`tools/github/\` executables |
| dispatcher.py | Entire 646-line file | Dead code | **Delete** |

## Cleanup plan

### P0 — Eliminate dead code + deduplicate

**1. Delete \`server/dispatcher.py\`** (646 lines)
- Fully replaced by \`foreman_agent.dispatch()\`
- Only referenced by its own tests (delete those too)

**2. Create \`server/config.py\`** (~60 lines)
- \`load_config()\`, \`save_config()\`, \`is_setup_complete()\`
- \`detect_repo_from_git()\`
- Replace all 3 copies + 2 repo-detection copies

**3. Move system prompt to \`server/foreman_prompt.md\`**
- Extract 106-line inline string
- Load at dispatch time (hot-reloadable)

### P1 — Generic tool bridge (kill 30 do_* functions)

**4. Agent gets 3 generic MCP tools:**
- \`run_tool(tool, executable, args)\` — runs \`tools/<tool>/<exec>.py\` via intercept
- \`run_shell(argv)\` — escape hatch via intercept
- \`get_budgets()\` — local read-only

**5. All gh operations become tools/github/ executables:**
- \`do_list_issues\` → already \`tools/github/list_issues.py\`
- \`do_comment_on_issue\` → new \`tools/github/comment_issue.py\`
- \`do_edit_issue\` → new \`tools/github/edit_issue.py\`
- \`do_view_issue\` → new \`tools/github/view_issue.py\`
- \`do_view_pr\` → new \`tools/github/view_pr.py\`
- \`do_reopen_issue\` → new \`tools/github/reopen_issue.py\`
- \`do_edit_project_field\` → new \`tools/github/edit_project_field.py\`
- \`do_list_project_items\` → new \`tools/github/list_project_items.py\`
- \`do_create_issue\` → already \`tools/github/open_issue.py\`

**6. Pipeline operations become tools/pipeline/**
- \`do_run_sync_issues\` → \`tools/pipeline/sync_issues.py\`
- \`do_run_heuristics\` → \`tools/pipeline/heuristics.py\`
- \`do_run_render_chart\` → \`tools/pipeline/render_chart.py\`
- \`do_run_estimate_dates\` → \`tools/pipeline/estimate_dates.py\`

**7. Control operations become tools/control/**
- \`do_loop_pause\` → \`tools/control/loop_pause.py\`
- \`do_loop_resume\` → \`tools/control/loop_resume.py\`
- \`do_loop_scope\` → \`tools/control/loop_scope.py\`
- \`do_get_budgets\` → \`tools/control/get_budgets.py\`

**8. Scenario operations become tools/scenarios/**
- \`do_start_scenario_session\` → \`tools/scenarios/start.py\`
- \`do_commit_scenario\` → \`tools/scenarios/commit.py\`
- etc.

**Impact:** foreman_agent.py goes from 1,673 to ~300 lines.

### P1.5 — UI extraction

**9. \`server/turn_ui.py\`**
- Move TurnUI, PlanItem, ToolTracker out of agent
- Agent stays UI-agnostic

**10. Single render pipeline**
- \`server/render_pipeline.py\` — one "HTML → screenshot → PNG" path
- Renderers with \`block_type\` auto-dispatch (no hardcoded mermaid)

**11. Per-conversation render folders**
\`\`\`
.imp/chats/
├── <id>.json        # session
└── <id>/            # rendered assets (PNG, HTML)
\`\`\`
- Scoped to conversation lifetime
- \`delete_session()\` removes both

### P2 — main.py cleanup

**12. Extract scenario UI** → \`server/scenario_ui.py\` (~483 lines)
**13. Delete stub responses** (~177 lines)
**14. Consolidate LLM backend call pattern** — single helper for guard, chat_history title generation

## Target state

\`\`\`
server/
├── config.py              # ONE config I/O + repo detection
├── foreman_agent.py       # ~300 lines: prompt + 3 tools + dispatch
├── foreman_prompt.md      # system prompt (file, not inline string)
├── turn_ui.py             # TurnUI ABC, PlanItem, ToolTracker
├── render_pipeline.py     # ONE render path
├── guard.py               # checkpoint B (no change)
├── intercept.py           # classify + execute (no change)
├── budgets.py             # counters (no change)
├── chat_history.py        # session persistence (no change)
├── data_layer.py          # Chainlit data layer (no change)
├── setup_agent.py         # setup flow (imports config.py)
├── render_route.py        # HTTP render server (no change)
├── screenshot.py          # html2image (no change)
└── md_postprocess.py      # block scanner (no change)
                           # dispatcher.py DELETED
\`\`\`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] \`server/dispatcher.py\` deleted (dead code)
- [ ] \`server/config.py\` exists, zero config/repo-detection duplication
- [ ] System prompt in file, not inline string
- [ ] Agent has 3 generic MCP tools, not 30 specific ones
- [ ] All gh/pipeline/control/scenario ops are tool executables
- [ ] TurnUI extracted from agent
- [ ] Single render pipeline
- [ ] Renderer watchdogs auto-dispatch
- [ ] Per-conversation render folders
- [ ] All existing tests pass (except deleted dispatcher tests)
- [ ] foreman_agent.py under 400 lines
- [ ] main.py under 1,500 lines

## Problem

When creating issue #71, the `create_issue` MCP tool:

1. **Has no `milestone` parameter** — forcing a workaround via `run_shell` with raw `gh issue create`
2. **Guard rejected valid labels** (e.g. `bug`) and milestone assignment on the first attempt, requiring a retry

This makes issue creation a multi-step pain:
- First call to `create_issue` fails or is incomplete
- Then need `run_shell` as escape hatch
- Or create bare issue + `edit_issue` to add milestone after the fact

## Expected behavior

- `create_issue` tool should accept a `milestone` parameter and pass it through to `gh issue create --milestone`
- Guard should allow standard labels (`bug`, `enhancement`, etc.) and milestone assignment when creating issues
- Single tool call should be sufficient to create a fully-formed issue

## Files likely involved

- `server/intercept.py` — tool parameter schema for `create_issue`
- `server/guard.py` — label/milestone validation rules
- MCP tool definitions

issues.body:
## Vision

Non-technical users build dashboards and automations by connecting tools through a shared **data table**. The table (`{columns, rows}`) is the universal interface — every tool reads from it, writes to it, and the user can always inspect and manually fix it without touching code.

The LLM helps wire things up from plain English. But when the LLM gets it wrong and you're on a deadline, you open the step, see the table, and fix it yourself — swap a column name, change a filter, reorder steps. Loose specs, tight interface.

## The DataFrame — tight interface

A strict rectangular table that flows through every workflow step via `context["data"]`.

### Shape rules
- **Always rectangular** — no ragged columns, no missing rows
- **Adding a column**: default-filled to match existing row count (default: `None`, configurable per column to `0`, `""`, last value, etc.)
- **Importing longer data**: column-by-column resolution prompt:
  - **Keep** (truncate to shortest)
  - **Pad with default** (fill shorter columns)
  - **Pad with last value** (repeat final value)
  - **Loop around** (cycle from start)
- Tools can never produce a broken table — the DataFrame enforces validity

### Format

```python
context["data"] = {
    "columns": ["date", "opened", "closed", "total"],
    "defaults": {"opened": 0, "closed": 0, "total": 0},  # per-column defaults
    "rows": [
        ["2026-04-14", 2, 5, 47],
        ["2026-04-15", 0, 0, 47],
        ["2026-04-16", 6, 6, 47],
    ]
}
```

### Why this format
- **Readable** — it's a spreadsheet you can eyeball at every step
- **Debuggable** — if step 3 outputs the wrong column, you see it in the step output
- **LLM-friendly** — named columns mean the LLM connects "make closed bars go down" to `closed` column + chart `direction: down`, not freeform code
- **No dependencies** — pure Python lists, serializes to JSON, optional pandas interop with one line
- Starts **empty** when workflow begins, persists in `last_run.json`

## User workflow

The office person who doesn't touch code:

1. **Picks tools** from the browser and drops them into a workflow
2. **Describes** what each step should do in plain text
3. **Clicks Done** → LLM wires up the columns and parameters
4. **Runs** → inspects the data table at each step
5. **Tweaks** — split the bars? adds a description change, LLM adjusts. Wrong column? fixes it manually in the table.
6. **A/B comparison** — copies the workflow, describes option B changes in English, LLM adjusts, runs both, compares charts
7. **Locks it in** — schedule the workflow, dashboard auto-updates

## Example: burndown chart

```
Step 1: list_issues        → [date, title, state, created_at, closed_at]
Step 2: group_by "date"    → [date, opened, closed]
Step 3: add_column "net"   → [date, opened, closed, net]        # net = opened - closed
Step 4: add_column "total" → [date, opened, closed, net, total] # running cumulative sum
Step 5: project_trend      → extends rows into future until total=0 based on pace
                              [date, opened, closed, net, total, projected]
Step 6: combo_chart        → dual bars (opened↑/closed↓) + line (total) + dashed (projected)
```

User says "make the projection show when we'll be done, not from the start" → LLM knows `project_trend` should only add rows after today. User sees the data table at step 5 and confirms the future dates are there.

## Tools

### Data producers (source → table)

Any existing tool that fetches data. Returns `{columns, rows}` format.
- `list_issues`, `list_prs`, etc. already exist — just need to output tabular data

### Data manipulation (table → table)

| Tool | What it does |
|------|-------------|
| `filter_rows` | Keep rows matching a condition |
| `filter_columns` | Keep/drop specific columns |
| `group_by` | Aggregate by column (count, sum, avg, min, max) |
| `sort` | Sort by column |
| `add_column` | Compute a new column from existing ones |
| `pivot` | Reshape rows↔columns |
| `project_trend` | Extrapolate future rows based on pace |

### Chart renderers (table → visual)

| Tool | Required columns | Optional |
|------|-----------------|----------|
| `bar_chart` | x, y | group_by, color, direction (up/down), stacked/grouped |
| `line_chart` | x, y | series, dashed |
| `histogram` | values | bins |
| `scatter_plot` | x, y | size, color |
| `combo_chart` | x + multiple y | per-series type (bar/line), dual axis |

Chart tools are **generic** — they read named columns, don't care about the data source. The user configures which columns map to which visual property.

### Parsers (raw output → table)

For upgrading legacy/raw output into the data format:

| Parser | Detects | Produces |
|--------|---------|----------|
| `parse_mermaid` | gantt/pie/bar blocks | `{columns, rows}` |
| `parse_csv` | CSV text | `{columns, rows}` |
| `parse_json` | JSON arrays/objects | `{columns, rows}` |
| `parse_table` | ASCII/markdown tables | `{columns, rows}` |

## Render path

Raw tool output gets upgraded to proper charts through a convert → render pipeline:

```
raw output → [Convert to Data] button → {columns, rows} → chart tool → rendered chart
```

1. **Tool runs** → output might contain mermaid, JSON, CSV, ASCII tables
2. **Detect** — parseable format detected → show **"Convert to Data"** button on step output
3. **Click** → parser extracts into `{columns, rows}`
4. **Chart step** → reads columns, renders Plotly/HTML chart
5. **User stays in control** — raw output preserved, button converts, doesn't destroy

The mermaid watchdog (P4.22) becomes `parse_mermaid` — one parser among many.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] DataFrame object with `{columns, rows, defaults}` — enforces rectangular shape
- [ ] Column add fills with default, length mismatch prompts resolution (keep/pad/last/loop)
- [ ] Workflow runner passes `context["data"]` between steps (starts empty)
- [ ] At least one data producer (e.g. `list_issues` outputs tabular data)
- [ ] At least one data manipulation tool (e.g. `filter_rows` or `group_by`)
- [ ] At least one chart tool (e.g. `bar_chart`) reads from data columns
- [ ] At least one parser tool (e.g. `parse_mermaid` or `parse_json`)
- [ ] "Convert to Data" button on step output when parseable format detected
- [ ] Data table visible and inspectable at every step in the UI
- [ ] Data persists in `last_run.json`
- [ ] Chart renders inline in workflow step output
- [ ] No pandas dependency in core — pure Python
- [ ] Pandas interop helper for tools that want it

## Supersedes
- Replaces the narrow bar chart overlap bug (original #71)
- The bar chart fix becomes a side effect of building proper chart tools

issues.body:
**Scope**

A hosted web service (the **Nexus**) where authors can create, edit,
and share tools and renderers. Backed by a database and a certificate
authority (CA) that uses asymmetric signatures — authors hold their
private key, consumers verify with the public part.

This is the network layer that turns the local tool repository (v0.2.1)
and signing model (v0.2.2) into a collaborative ecosystem.

## Architecture

\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Nexus Server                  │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  │
│  │  Web API  │  │    CA    │  │   Database    │  │
│  │ (FastAPI) │  │ (signing │  │ (tools, users │  │
│  │           │  │  verify) │  │  signatures)  │  │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └───────────────┘  │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │          Tool/Renderer Registry          │    │
│  │  browse · search · download · contribute │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▲                           ▲
         │ publish (signed)          │ install (verify)
         │                           │
    ┌────┴─────┐               ┌─────┴────┐
    │  Author  │               │ Consumer │
    │ (priv key)│               │ (pub key)│
    └──────────┘               └──────────┘
\`\`\`

## Certificate Authority

### Key model
- **Author registration**: generates an asymmetric keypair (Ed25519).
  Private key stays with the author. Public key + identity registered
  in the Nexus database.
- **Tool signing**: when an author publishes a tool/renderer, the
  package is signed with their private key. The signature + public key
  fingerprint are stored in the database.
- **Consumer verification**: downloading a tool verifies the signature
  against the author's registered public key. Tampered packages fail
  verification.

### Identity
- Authors are identified by their public key fingerprint
  (e.g. \`imp:ed25519:abc123def456\`)
- Optional: link to GitHub identity for trust anchoring
  (\`gh:KKallas\` → verified via OAuth)
- The Nexus acts as the CA — it doesn't hold private keys, just
  registers public keys and maintains the trust chain

## Database schema

\`\`\`sql
-- Authors (registered identities)
authors (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  fingerprint   TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,    -- ed25519 public key fingerprint
  display_name  TEXT,
  github_id     TEXT,                    -- optional GitHub identity
  public_key    BYTEA NOT NULL,          -- raw public key bytes
  registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  last_active   TIMESTAMPTZ
)

-- Published packages (tools + renderers)
packages (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  author_id     UUID REFERENCES authors,
  name          TEXT NOT NULL,            -- e.g. "github/moderate_issues"
  kind          TEXT NOT NULL,            -- "tool" | "renderer"
  version       TEXT NOT NULL,            -- semver
  description   TEXT,
  readme        TEXT,
  manifest_json JSONB NOT NULL,           -- package manifest
  signature     BYTEA NOT NULL,           -- author's Ed25519 signature
  checksum      TEXT NOT NULL,            -- SHA-256 of package content
  published_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  downloads     INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
  UNIQUE (author_id, name, version)
)

-- Package files (blobs stored alongside metadata)
package_files (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  package_id    UUID REFERENCES packages,
  path          TEXT NOT NULL,            -- relative path within package
  content       BYTEA NOT NULL,
  size          INTEGER NOT NULL
)

-- Trust chain (who trusts whom)
trust_links (
  truster_id    UUID REFERENCES authors,
  trustee_id    UUID REFERENCES authors,
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (truster_id, trustee_id)
)
\`\`\`

## Web API

### Auth
- \`POST /auth/register\` — register author (public key + identity)
- \`POST /auth/verify\` — verify ownership (sign a challenge nonce)
- \`GET  /auth/whoami\` — current author identity

### Packages
- \`GET    /packages\` — browse/search (filterable by kind, author, name)
- \`GET    /packages/{author}/{name}\` — package detail + versions
- \`GET    /packages/{author}/{name}/{version}/download\` — download + verify
- \`POST   /packages\` — publish (signed package upload)
- \`PUT    /packages/{author}/{name}/{version}\` — update (re-sign)
- \`DELETE /packages/{author}/{name}/{version}\` — unpublish

### Authors
- \`GET  /authors\` — list registered authors
- \`GET  /authors/{fingerprint}\` — author profile + packages

## CLI integration

From the Imp client side, new commands:

\`\`\`bash
# Author setup (one-time)
imp nexus register          # generate keypair, register with Nexus
imp nexus login              # verify identity (sign challenge)

# Publishing
imp nexus publish tools/github/    # sign + upload a tool
imp nexus publish renderers/mermaid/  # sign + upload a renderer

# Consuming
imp nexus search "github"    # search packages
imp nexus install author/tool  # download + verify + install locally
imp nexus update              # update all installed packages
\`\`\`

These commands wrap the Web API and handle signing/verification
locally using the author's private key.

## Relationship to existing v0.2.x

| Issue | What it provides | This issue builds on |
|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| #65 (v0.2.0) | Package folder format | The on-disk format packages are uploaded in |
| #48 (v0.2.1) | Local vector index | Local search; Nexus adds network search |
| #49 (v0.2.2) | Identity + signing model | The CA model; Nexus hosts the public keys |
| **This (v0.2.4)** | **Web server + DB + sharing** | **The network service** |
| #50 (v0.2.3→v0.2.5) | Governance | Builds on the Nexus for copy-detection, takedown |

## References

- #49 — v0.2.2 central registry (identity model this builds on)
- #50 — v0.2.3 quality & governance (builds on this)
- #48 — v0.2.1 local tool repository
- #65 — v0.2.0 package layout
- #68 — P4.26 toolset CRUD (the local tools this shares)

## Files touched

- \`nexus/\` — **new** — Nexus server package
  - \`nexus/app.py\` — FastAPI web server
  - \`nexus/db.py\` — database models (SQLAlchemy or similar)
  - \`nexus/ca.py\` — certificate authority (Ed25519 signing/verification)
  - \`nexus/api/\` — route handlers
- \`tools/nexus/\` — **new** — CLI commands (\`register\`, \`publish\`, \`install\`)
- \`server/foreman_agent.py\` — Foreman tools for browsing/installing from Nexus

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Web server starts and serves the API on a configurable port
- [ ] Author registration generates Ed25519 keypair, stores public key in DB
- [ ] Tool/renderer publishing signs the package and uploads to Nexus
- [ ] Package download verifies signature against author's registered public key
- [ ] Tampered packages fail verification with a clear error
- [ ] Search by name, author, kind works
- [ ] CLI commands (\`register\`, \`publish\`, \`install\`) work end-to-end
- [ ] Database persists across server restarts

## Out of scope (defer to v0.2.5/governance)

- Automated copy-detection (defer to #50)
- Takedown requests (defer to #50)
- Reputation scoring (defer to #50)
- Package reviews/ratings
- Paid/premium packages

issues.body:
**Scope**

Two UX problems with the mermaid/chart rendering flow:

1. **No progress indicator during rendering**: After Foreman finishes
   its answer, the mermaid screenshot takes several seconds. During this
   time the user sees nothing — just the stop button instead of submit,
   with no indication that rendering is happening. Need a visible
   placeholder like \`[Rendering chart...]\` that appears immediately
   and gets replaced by the PNG when ready.

2. **"Foreman is thinking" stays visible after thinking ends**: The
   status line \`Used / Foreman is thinking...\` appears right after the
   query and pushes content off-screen. When thinking stops and the
   answer streams in, the "thinking" text remains — stale and
   misleading.

3. **No visible activity at the bottom of the chat**: While Foreman
   works (tool calls, rendering, etc.), the user should always see
   something at the last visible line indicating work is in progress.
   Currently the action happens above the fold and the user stares at
   a blank area wondering if the app froze.

## Proposed fixes

### 1. Rendering placeholder

When the watchdog detects a mermaid block, immediately insert a
placeholder message before starting the screenshot:

\`\`\`
[Rendering mermaid diagram...]
\`\`\`

Then replace it with the PNG + viewer link when the screenshot
completes. If the screenshot fails, replace with the raw code block
+ error note.

This applies to all renderable code blocks (mermaid, plotly, etc.),
not just mermaid.

### 2. Fix stale "thinking" indicator

- Clear the "Foreman is thinking..." status line as soon as the first
  answer token streams in (not when the full answer completes)
- If tool calls are running, show which tool is active instead of
  generic "thinking" (this may already work via the P4.23 plan
  checklist — verify)

### 3. Bottom-of-chat activity indicator

Always keep something visible at the last line of the chat while
Foreman is working:

- During tool calls: the plan checklist (P4.23) already shows this
- During answer streaming: the streaming text itself
- During post-processing (screenshots): the \`[Rendering...]\`
  placeholder
- During title generation: nothing visible — add a subtle indicator

The key invariant: **the user should never see a static screen with
only the stop button and no visible progress for more than 1-2
seconds.**

## References

- #60 — P4.24 renderer plugin system
- #55 — P4.23 structured turn UI (plan checklist, streamed reasoning)
- \`main.py:_apply_mermaid_watchdog\` — current watchdog
- \`main.py:_ForemanTurnUI\` — structured turn rendering

## Files touched

- \`main.py\` — rendering placeholder, thinking indicator cleanup,
  bottom-of-chat activity
- \`server/md_postprocess.py\` — placeholder injection before screenshot

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] \`[Rendering chart...]\` placeholder appears immediately when a
      renderable code block is detected
- [ ] Placeholder replaced by PNG when screenshot completes
- [ ] "Foreman is thinking" clears when first answer token arrives
- [ ] User always sees activity at the bottom of the visible chat area
- [ ] No static screen with only stop button for more than 1-2 seconds
- [ ] Works for all renderable types (mermaid, plotly, etc.)
