issues.body:
**Scope**

Define the toolset data model: each **tool** is a folder containing a
fixed **script** (the executor) plus zero or more **stored executables**
(prompts, configs, parameter sets) that the script operates on.  The
framework provides a standard CRUD lifecycle for the stored executables,
with \`new\` and \`delete\` as reserved operations.

## Data model

\`\`\`
tools/<tool-name>/
├── README.md              # tool documentation
├── script.py              # the executor (fixed, one per tool)
├── executables/            # stored executables (CRUD-managed)
│   ├── default.md          # a stored executable (prompt/config)
│   ├── strict_review.md    # another stored executable
│   └── lenient_triage.md   # etc.
└── tests/
    └── ...
\`\`\`

### Terminology

| Concept | Example | Managed by |
|---------|---------|------------|
| **Tool** | \`moderate_issues\` | Developer (folder structure) |
| **Script** | \`moderate_issues/script.py\` | Developer (code) |
| **Executable** | \`moderate_issues/executables/strict_review.md\` | Admin (CRUD via Foreman) |

A **tool** is the capability (e.g. "moderate issues").  The **script**
is the fixed code that does the work.  An **executable** is a stored
configuration/prompt that parameterises how the script runs — like a
saved preset.

### Example: moderate_issues

\`\`\`
tools/moderate_issues/
├── README.md
├── script.py                     # the moderation engine
├── executables/
│   ├── default.md                # standard moderation prompt
│   ├── strict_security.md        # security-focused review
│   └── quick_triage.md           # lightweight triage pass
└── tests/
\`\`\`

Running \`moderate_issues\` with the \`strict_security\` executable means
the script loads \`strict_security.md\` as its prompt/config and runs
with those parameters.

## CRUD lifecycle

### Reserved operations

\`new\` and \`delete\` are **reserved** — they're framework operations,
not tool-specific executables:

| Operation | What it does | Who calls it |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| **new** | Create a new executable in the tool's \`executables/\` folder | Admin via Foreman |
| **delete** | Remove a stored executable | Admin via Foreman |
| **edit** | Modify an existing executable in-place | Admin via Foreman |
| **list** | Show all stored executables for a tool | Admin via Foreman |
| **run** | Execute the script with a specific executable | Foreman (autonomous or manual) |

### Foreman MCP tools

\`\`\`
tool_list_executables(tool_name) → [{name, path, size, modified_at}]
tool_new_executable(tool_name, exec_name, content) → {path}
tool_edit_executable(tool_name, exec_name, content) → {path}
tool_delete_executable(tool_name, exec_name) → {deleted: bool}
tool_run(tool_name, exec_name, **kwargs) → {exit_code, output}
\`\`\`

### Validation

- Executable names are filesystem-safe (alphanumeric + underscores +
  hyphens, no path separators)
- \`new\` fails if the name already exists
- \`delete\` fails if the executable doesn't exist
- \`edit\` fails if the executable doesn't exist (use \`new\` to create)
- The script itself is never modified via CRUD — only executables

## Migration from current structure

| Current | Becomes |
|---------|---------|
| \`99-tools/moderate_issues.py\` | \`tools/moderate_issues/script.py\` |
| \`99-tools/moderate_issues.md\` | \`tools/moderate_issues/executables/default.md\` |
| \`99-tools/fix_prs.py\` | \`tools/fix_prs/script.py\` |
| \`99-tools/fix_prs_analysis.md\` | \`tools/fix_prs/executables/analysis.md\` |
| \`99-tools/fix_prs_fix.md\` | \`tools/fix_prs/executables/fix.md\` |
| \`99-tools/solve_issues.py\` | \`tools/solve_issues/script.py\` |
| \`99-tools/solve_issues.md\` | \`tools/solve_issues/executables/default.md\` |
| \`99-tools/project_description.md\` | \`tools/shared/project_description.md\` (shared across tools) |

## References

- #51 — P4.21 tool folder structure (parallel effort)
- #60 — P4.24 renderers (same folder-per-plugin pattern)
- #48 — v0.2.1 Tool Repository (future: vector index, contribute)

## Files touched

- \`tools/__init__.py\` — **new** — tool discovery + CRUD helpers
- \`tools/<name>/\` — **new** — one folder per tool
- \`server/foreman_agent.py\` — register CRUD MCP tools
- \`server/intercept.py\` — update tool execution paths
- \`99-tools/\` — retired (contents migrated)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Each tool is a self-contained folder with script + executables/ + README
- [ ] \`new\` and \`delete\` are reserved framework operations, not executable names
- [ ] Foreman can list, create, edit, delete, and run executables via MCP tools
- [ ] Existing tools (\`moderate_issues\`, \`fix_prs\`, \`solve_issues\`) migrated
- [ ] Executable names validated (filesystem-safe, no collisions with reserved ops)
- [ ] Tool discovery scans \`tools/*/script.py\`

## Out of scope

- Version history for executables (just overwrite on edit for now)
- Executable sharing across tools (each tool owns its own)
- GUI editor for executables (Foreman text-based CRUD is sufficient)
issues.title: [P4.26] toolset — tool script + stored executables with CRUD lifecycle

issues.body:
**Problem**

When Foreman calls `run_shell` with a multiline script (e.g.
`python3 -c "<50 lines of inline code>"`), the entire script body
lives only inside the `-c` string argument. It is:

- **not inspectable after the fact** — the `.imp/output/<id>.log`
  captures stdout/stderr but not the source code that produced it
- **not re-runnable** — you can't `python3 .imp/output/<id>.py` to
  reproduce the result
- **hard to review in Guard** — Guard sees a wall of escaped code
  inside a JSON argv array instead of a clean script file

**Proposed behaviour**

When `intercept.execute_command` detects a multiline `-c` pattern
(heuristic: `argv` matches `[python3, -c, <body>]` or
`[bash, -c, <body>]` and `<body>` contains `\n`), it should:

1. **Write the script** to `.imp/output/<action_id>.{py,sh}` (same
   directory as the log, extension matching the interpreter).
2. **Rewrite argv** from `[python3, -c, <body>]` to
   `[python3, .imp/output/<action_id>.py]` before spawning the
   subprocess.
3. **Include the script path** in the tool result dict returned by
   `_shell_result`, alongside the existing `action_id` — so the
   LLM and UI can link to it the same way logs are linked today.

The script file should be subject to the same 100 MB rotation as
log files (already handled by `_rotate_output_dir`).

**Example**

Before (current):
```
run_shell(argv=['python3', '-c', "\nimport json, os, pathlib\n...50 lines..."])
→ .imp/output/act_a1b2c3d4.log   (stdout only)
```

After:
```
run_shell(argv=['python3', '-c', "\nimport json, os, pathlib\n...50 lines..."])
→ .imp/output/act_a1b2c3d4.py    (the script itself)
→ .imp/output/act_a1b2c3d4.log   (stdout/stderr as before)
→ tool result includes {"script_file": ".imp/output/act_a1b2c3d4.py", ...}
```

**Scope**

- `server/intercept.py` — detect multiline `-c`, write script file,
  rewrite argv
- `server/foreman_agent.py` — `_shell_result` returns `script_file`
  path when present
- Script file reuses the existing `action_id` naming and output
  directory

**Acceptance criteria**

- [ ] `python3 -c "<multiline>"` creates `.imp/output/<id>.py` and
  executes via `python3 .imp/output/<id>.py`
- [ ] `bash -c "<multiline>"` creates `.imp/output/<id>.sh` and
  executes via `bash .imp/output/<id>.sh`
- [ ] Single-line `-c` scripts (no `\n`) are left inline — no file
  created for trivial one-liners
- [ ] `_shell_result` includes `script_file` key when a script was
  persisted
- [ ] Script files are cleaned up by `_rotate_output_dir` alongside
  logs
- [ ] Guard still sees the full script content for review (either
  by reading the file or from the original argv — whichever is
  simpler)

**References**

- KKallas/Imp#46 — Guard code-review checklist for `python3 -c` /
  `bash -c`
- KKallas/Imp#21 — register pipeline tools in foreman
- `server/intercept.py` — `execute_command`, `_log_file_for`,
  `_rotate_output_dir`
- `server/foreman_agent.py` — `do_run_shell`, `_shell_result`

issues.body:
## Vision

Standardize how tools and renderers are stored locally so every package is a self-contained, verifiable folder. This is the **on-disk format** that v0.2.1 (vector index), v0.2.2 (central registry + certs), and v0.2.3 (governance) all build on top of.

## Current state

Flat layout, no ownership, no integrity:

```
renderers/
  mermaid/
    renderer.py
    template.html.j2
  bar/
    renderer.py
    template.html.j2

99-tools/
  fix_prs.py
  solve_issues.py
```

## Target layout

```
packages/
  <owner>/
    <tool-name>/
      README.md          # what it does, args, examples, license
      manifest.json      # metadata (see below)
      checksums.sha256   # sha256 of every file in the package
      signature.sig      # detached signature over checksums.sha256
      <internals>/       # the actual code, templates, assets
        renderer.py
        template.html.j2
        ...
```

### Example

```
packages/
  imp-official/
    mermaid-renderer/
      README.md
      manifest.json
      checksums.sha256
      signature.sig
      renderer.py
      template.html.j2
  kkallas/
    fix-prs/
      README.md
      manifest.json
      checksums.sha256
      signature.sig
      fix_prs.py
      fix_prs_analysis.md
      fix_prs_fix.md
```

## manifest.json

```json
{
  "name": "mermaid-renderer",
  "owner": "imp-official",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "renderer",
  "description": "Renders Mermaid diagrams to interactive HTML + PNG screenshot",
  "license": "MIT",
  "entry_point": "renderer.py",
  "args_schema": { ... },
  "created_at": "2026-04-17T00:00:00Z",
  "updated_at": "2026-04-17T00:00:00Z",
  "parent": null,
  "imp_version_min": "0.2.0"
}
```

## Integrity: checksums + signature

### checksums.sha256
Generated by hashing every file in the package (excluding `checksums.sha256` and `signature.sig` themselves):

```
a1b2c3d4...  README.md
e5f6a7b8...  manifest.json
c9d0e1f2...  renderer.py
3a4b5c6d...  template.html.j2
```

### signature.sig
Detached signature over `checksums.sha256`.

**Phase 1 (this issue):** signed with a local keypair (`~/.imp/keys/`). Generated on first run or `imp keys init`. Self-signed — proves the package hasn't been tampered with since the signer created it, but doesn't prove WHO the signer is.

**Phase 2 (v0.2.2):** signed with the short-lived agent cert from the central CA. Proves both integrity AND identity.

## Validation before install

When Imp loads a package (at startup or on install):

1. Recompute sha256 of every file → compare against `checksums.sha256`
2. Verify `signature.sig` against `checksums.sha256` using the signer's public key
3. If either fails → refuse to load, warn the user

This makes supply-chain tampering detectable even without a central server.

## CLI commands

```bash
imp package init              # scaffold a new package in current dir
imp package validate <path>   # check checksums + signature
imp package sign <path>       # (re)generate checksums + sign
imp package install <path>    # copy to packages/, validate, register
imp package list              # list installed packages by owner/name
```

## Migration path

- Built-in renderers and tools move to `packages/imp-official/`
- Old `renderers/` and `99-tools/` paths supported as fallback during transition
- `imp migrate-packages` command converts old layout → new layout

## Relationship to v0.2.x

| Issue | What it adds on top |
|---|---|
| **This (v0.2.0)** | Folder layout, manifest, checksums, local-key signature |
| v0.2.1 | Vector index over package manifests for search |
| v0.2.2 | Central CA replaces local keys; network sync |
| v0.2.3 | Copy-detection, takedown, reputation across the corpus |

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `packages/<owner>/<tool>/` folder convention enforced
- [ ] `manifest.json` schema defined and validated on load
- [ ] `README.md` required in every package
- [ ] `checksums.sha256` generated from all package files
- [ ] `signature.sig` generated with local keypair; verified on load
- [ ] Local keypair generation (`~/.imp/keys/`) on first run or `imp keys init`
- [ ] Validation runs at package load time; tampered packages refused with clear error
- [ ] CLI: `imp package {init,validate,sign,install,list}`
- [ ] Migration tool: `imp migrate-packages` converts `renderers/` + `99-tools/` → new layout
- [ ] Existing renderer/tool loading works unchanged during transition (fallback to old paths)
- [ ] Tests:
  - Checksum generation + verification round-trip
  - Tampered file detected and refused
  - Invalid/missing signature detected and refused
  - `manifest.json` schema validation (missing fields, bad types)
  - Migration tool preserves functionality

## Out of scope (v0.2.2)
- Certificate-authority-signed packages (central CA)
- Network fetch / sync
- Owner identity verification beyond local keypair
issues.title: [v0.2.0] Package layout — owner/tool folders with README, signature & checksum

issues.body:
## Vision

Imp lives in a single folder you can download and drop into any directory. From there it bootstraps an entire software project from zero to shipped.

## How it works

### 1. Drop Imp into a folder
Download the Imp folder into any target directory. Target can be:
- **Empty folder** — greenfield project
- **Partial prototype** — existing code without repo/structure
- **Existing git repo** — already has history

### 2. Imp detects context and adapts

| Starting state | What Imp does |
|---|---|
| Empty folder | Asks for project vision via conversation. Suggests name, description, license. Creates GitHub repo. |
| Partial code | Detects language/framework/structure. Identifies gaps (no README, no tests, no CI). Offers to fill them. Pushes to GitHub if not already a repo. |
| Existing repo | Connects to remote. Reads issues, PRs, commit history. Understands current state. |

### 3. Set up project management
- Creates GitHub issue labels and milestones
- Optionally connects to external trackers (Linear, Jira) if configured

### 4. Spec → milestones → issues
- Asks user for initial spec (conversation, paste, or document)
- Breaks spec into milestones with clear deliverables
- Breaks milestones into sized issues with acceptance criteria
- User reviews and approves the plan

### 5. Work through issues
- Picks up issues in priority order
- Implements, tests, creates PRs
- Asks for review/clarification when stuck
- Updates project board as work progresses
- Repeats until project is done

## Key principles
- **Single folder** — no global install, no system dependencies beyond Python/Node
- **Works offline-first** — local LLM fallback for basic operations
- **Opinionated defaults, escapable** — picks sensible license/CI/structure but lets you override everything
- **Transparent** — every automated action is logged and reversible

## Relationship to current Imp
This is the end-state vision. Current Imp already handles steps 4–5 (spec → issues → implementation) for an existing repo. This issue tracks expanding to cover steps 1–3 (bootstrapping from nothing).
issues.title: [v1.0] Drop-in project bootstrapper — download Imp, point at folder, ship software

issues.body:
## Vision

Pippi Longstocking-themed marketing campaign for Imp. Core message: the tool does the work while you live your life.

### TikTok / shaky-phone series (15–30s each)
Shot like a coworker filming in disbelief. Lower-third caption + timestamp.

1. **Spa** — colleague in robe, cucumbers on eyes, laptop closed. Cut to Slack: "report attached 🌸"
2. **Gym** — deadlift PR, no laptop. Cut to manager: "cleanest deck this quarter."
3. **Sunbathing** — beach, spritz, sunglasses. Cut to calendar: deadline tomorrow → ✅ submitted.
4. **Run** — 10k along river. Phone buzzes: "promotion conversation?"
5. **Reveal** (pinned) — same person at desk at 5am with Imp running. Caption: "the secret is I didn't."

### Guerrilla mural (2–3 layer stencil + QR)
- Pippi as office secretary — braids sideways, striped stockings under pencil skirt
- One hand types, the other lifts entire desk above her head (horse callback)
- Mr. Nilsson on the monitor wearing a tiny tie
- Clock at 4:59, calendar with tomorrow circled, inbox tray labeled "DONE"
- **Layers:** black (outlines), red (braids, stripes, deadline), yellow (freckles, highlights)
- QR as a Post-it note on the lifted desk → landing page

### Merch
- **T-shirt front:** Mr. Nilsson in a tie holding a banana like a pen, on a stack of reports. Caption: **"PAY PEANUTS. GET MONKEYS."**
- **Back:** `imp.sh` + tiny QR
- Variants: "MY MONKEY DID IT" / "EMPLOYEE OF THE MONTH" with Nilsson photo

### Tagline candidates
- "Work hard. Or don't."
- "The report wrote itself."
- "She doesn't work here. It works for her."

### Legal notes
- Lindgren estate is litigious — visual homage in mural OK, commercial character-name use needs review
- "Secretly filmed" aesthetic: cast deliberately, label #ad, keep the vibe

issues.body:
**Scope**

The chat sidebar is broken in several ways: messages vanish on page
refresh, empty stub sessions appear unprompted, and the UX around
creating vs resuming chats is confusing. This issue fixes the
persistence model and simplifies the sidebar to behave predictably.

**Priority**: before all other open issues — the admin can't trust the
tool if their conversation disappears on refresh.

## Current problems

### 1. Text goes missing on page refresh

When the browser tab is refreshed:

1. `cl.user_session` (in-memory dict) is wiped by Chainlit.
2. `@cl.on_chat_start` fires and calls `_start_new_chat_session()`,
   which generates a **new** UUID and writes a new stub file —
   instead of resuming the session that was just active.
3. The old session's file is on disk but the in-memory reference is
   gone. `_current_session()` returns `None` or points at the new
   empty stub, so subsequent messages are orphaned or lost.

Additionally, if the page refreshes mid-dispatch (after user turn is
appended but before assistant reply is), the assistant response is
never persisted — the turn is recorded but the response is lost.

**Root**: `main.py:1012-1013` — `_current_session()` returns None
when `cl.user_session` has been cleared by Chainlit on reconnect.

### 2. Random empty chats appearing in sidebar

`data_layer.py:update_thread()` (lines 107-119) creates a 237-byte
stub `ChatSession` every time Chainlit calls it — even for sessions
that were never explicitly started by the user. These stub files
(zero turns, no title) appear in the sidebar listing alongside real
conversations.

**Root**: `data_layer.py:113` creates a stub on every
`update_thread()` call if the session doesn't exist on disk.

### 3. No default "resume previous" behaviour

On page load, the app always enters `@cl.on_chat_start` which creates
a fresh session. The user has to manually click a past chat in the
sidebar to resume it. There's no concept of "reopen the last active
chat."

## Proposed behaviour

### Default to resume

- On page load, automatically resume the **most recent** chat session
  (by `last_active_at`).
- User explicitly creates a new chat via the "New Chat" button (the
  `confirm_new_chat = true` config is already set).
- User can delete old chats from the sidebar.

### No stub creation

- `update_thread()` should **not** create stub files. Only
  `_start_new_chat_session()` (explicit new chat) creates a session
  file.
- On startup, prune orphaned stubs (files with zero turns and no
  `last_active_at` within the last hour).

### Date-stamped titles in sidebar

When a new chat is created, its sidebar title should use the format:

```
[Apr 17 14:32] Chat title here
```

The date-time prefix should be visually smaller / muted compared to
the title text. If Chainlit's sidebar doesn't support rich formatting,
use the plain format above — the brackets + short date keep it
compact.

### Simplify persistence

The full conversation text is what gets sent to Claude on each turn.
The chat session file on disk is a convenience for sidebar listing
and resume — not the source of truth for the LLM context. Given
this:

- Drop the `chat-XXXX` synthetic ID scheme. Use Chainlit's
  `thread_id` directly as the session ID to avoid ID mismatches
  between Chainlit's in-memory state and our on-disk files.
- Keep the JSON file as the persistence layer (atomic writes via
  `.tmp` + `rename` are already correct) but align the file naming
  to use `thread_id` as the primary key.

## References

- Current implementation: `server/chat_history.py` (509 lines),
  `server/data_layer.py`, `main.py:456-503` (lifecycle),
  `main.py:1012-1039` (turn persistence)
- Chainlit config: `.chainlit/config.toml` (`session_timeout=3600`,
  `confirm_new_chat=true`)
- Chat files: `.imp/chats/*.json`

## Files touched

- `server/chat_history.py` — session resume logic, drop synthetic IDs,
  date-stamped titles, stub pruning
- `server/data_layer.py` — stop creating stubs in `update_thread()`
- `main.py` — `on_chat_start` resumes last session, `on_chat_resume`
  simplified

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Page refresh resumes the same chat — no new stub created, no
      messages lost
- [ ] Sidebar shows only real conversations (no empty stubs)
- [ ] New chats require explicit "New Chat" click
- [ ] Sidebar titles show `[date time] title` format
- [ ] `update_thread()` never creates stub files
- [ ] Orphaned stubs pruned on startup
- [ ] Assistant replies mid-dispatch survive page refresh (saved
      before dispatch, updated after)
- [ ] Chat ID uses Chainlit's `thread_id` directly — no synthetic
      `chat-XXXX`

## Out of scope

- Multi-user chat isolation (single-admin app)
- Chat export / import
- Search within past chats

milestone.title: V0.1
milestone.description: All phases (1-7) consolidated into a single v0.1 release milestone

issues.body:
**Scope**

Add a rendering service that turns parameterised HTML templates into
PNG screenshots for inline chat display, while keeping the interactive
HTML one click away. This closes the gap where Mermaid and Plotly
output currently shows as raw code or doesn't render inline — instead,
every visualisation type gets a static preview image in the chat message
and a link to the live interactive page.

The system has three layers:

1. **Template service** — Jinja2 HTML templates that accept data via
   URL query parameters (`?data=...&title=...`). Served by a lightweight
   route on the Chainlit server.
2. **Screenshot engine** — headless browser (Playwright) that renders
   the template URL to a PNG and returns the image bytes.
3. **Renderer plugins** — a folder structure (`renderers/<type>/`) where
   each plugin is a fully self-contained folder with its own template,
   renderer logic, assets, and tests.

## URL contract

```
GET /render/<type>?var1=val1&var2=val2             → PNG image (default)
GET /render/<type>?var1=val1&var2=val2&mode=viewer → interactive HTML page
```

When `mode` is omitted (or `mode=image`), the server renders the HTML
template, screenshots it via Playwright, and returns `image/png`. When
`mode=viewer`, it returns the live HTML directly — same template, same
data, but the user gets the interactive version (hover, zoom, click).

This means a single URL encodes both the static preview and the
interactive experience. Chat messages embed:

```markdown
[![Gantt chart](https://…/render/gantt?data=…)](https://…/render/gantt?data=…&mode=viewer)
```

Image in the chat, click opens the interactive page.

## Renderer plugin structure

Every visualisation type is a **self-contained folder** — template,
renderer logic, assets, and tests all live together. No split between
a `renderers/` tree and a separate `templates/` tree. Each folder is
the single source of truth for that chart type.

```
renderers/
├── __init__.py              # plugin discovery (scan subdirs for renderer.py)
├── base.py                  # BaseRenderer ABC + shared helpers
│
├── mermaid/                 # generic mermaid diagrams
│   ├── renderer.py          # parse ```mermaid block → template vars
│   ├── template.html.j2     # standalone HTML that loads mermaid.min.js
│   └── test_mermaid.py
│
├── plotly/                  # raw plotly JSON figures
│   ├── renderer.py          # parse plotly JSON → template vars
│   ├── template.html.j2     # standalone HTML with plotly.js CDN
│   └── test_plotly.py
│
├── gantt/                   # gantt timeline (migrated from templates/gantt.html.j2)
│   ├── renderer.py          # wraps existing build_context_for_gantt()
│   ├── template.html.j2     # moved from templates/gantt.html.j2
│   ├── sample.json          # example input for manual testing
│   └── test_gantt.py
│
├── burndown/                # burndown chart
│   ├── renderer.py          # wraps existing build_context_for_burndown()
│   ├── template.html.j2     # moved from templates/burndown.html.j2
│   └── test_burndown.py
│
├── kanban/                  # kanban board
│   ├── renderer.py          # wraps existing build_context_for_kanban()
│   ├── template.html.j2     # moved from templates/kanban.html.j2
│   └── test_kanban.py
│
├── comparison/              # side-by-side scenario comparison
│   ├── renderer.py          # wraps existing build_context_for_comparison()
│   ├── template.html.j2     # moved from templates/comparison.html.j2
│   └── test_comparison.py
│
├── scatter/                 # scatter plot (new)
│   ├── renderer.py
│   ├── template.html.j2
│   └── test_scatter.py
│
└── bar/                     # bar graph (new)
    ├── renderer.py
    ├── template.html.j2
    └── test_bar.py
```

The existing `templates/` folder is retired — its `.html.j2` files
move into their respective `renderers/<type>/` folders. `fields.json`
moves to `renderers/fields.json` (shared schema).

Each `renderer.py` exposes a class implementing `BaseRenderer`:

```python
# renderers/base.py
class BaseRenderer(ABC):
    name: str                              # folder name, used in /render/<name>
    block_type: str | None = None          # fenced code-block lang tag (None = not triggered by markdown blocks)

    @abstractmethod
    def parse(self, raw: str | dict) -> dict:
        """Extract template variables from raw input (markdown block or JSON)."""

    def template_path(self) -> Path:
        """Default: template.html.j2 in the same folder as renderer.py."""
        return Path(__file__).parent / self.name / "template.html.j2"

    def build_url(self, params: dict, base: str) -> str:
        """Build the /render/... URL with encoded params."""
        ...
```

```python
# renderers/mermaid/renderer.py
class MermaidRenderer(BaseRenderer):
    name = "mermaid"
    block_type = "mermaid"

    def parse(self, raw: str) -> dict:
        return {"diagram": raw}
```

### Adding a new chart type

To add a new visualisation (e.g. `heatmap`):

1. Create `renderers/heatmap/`
2. Add `renderer.py` with a class extending `BaseRenderer`
3. Add `template.html.j2` with the HTML/JS to render it
4. Optional: add `test_heatmap.py` and `sample.json`

Plugin discovery in `renderers/__init__.py` auto-scans subdirectories
for `renderer.py` — no central registry to update.

## Markdown post-processor

A function in `server/` that:

1. Scans the assistant's markdown output for fenced code blocks
   matching a registered renderer's `block_type`
2. Calls `renderer.parse(block)` → template vars
3. Calls the screenshot service → PNG bytes → saves to `public/images/`
4. Replaces the code block with `[![alt](image_url)](viewer_url)`

This runs as a post-processing step before the message is sent to
Chainlit, so the user sees a rendered image instead of raw syntax.

## Screenshot engine

```python
# server/screenshot.py
async def screenshot(renderer_name: str, params: dict, *, width=1200, height=800) -> bytes:
    """Render a template URL in headless Chromium and return PNG bytes."""
    url = f"http://localhost:{PORT}/render/{renderer_name}?{urlencode(params)}"
    async with async_playwright() as p:
        browser = await p.chromium.launch()
        page = await browser.new_page(viewport={"width": width, "height": height})
        await page.goto(url, wait_until="networkidle")
        png = await page.screenshot(full_page=True)
        await browser.close()
    return png
```

Cache the browser instance across calls to avoid cold-start on every
render. Consider a process-level singleton with a warm Chromium
instance that resets between screenshots.

## Integration with existing pipeline

- **`render_chart.py`** — existing builder functions
  (`build_context_for_gantt()`, etc.) are wrapped by each plugin's
  `renderer.py`. The builders stay in `render_chart.py` as shared
  logic; the plugin just calls them and returns template vars.
  Over time the builders can migrate into their plugin folders.
- **`main.py:_render_chart_file()`** — instead of only doing
  `cl.Plotly()` for burndown and markdown links for others, all
  types get a screenshot preview + interactive link.
- **`mermaid_to_plotly.py`** — the mermaid renderer plugin replaces
  this workaround. Mermaid blocks render natively via the template
  service screenshot rather than being converted to Plotly.

## References

- #55 — Foreman turn UI (the context where inline rendering matters)
- #24 — Chainlit control surfaces (will consume rendered images)
- Existing templates: `templates/gantt.html.j2`, `burndown.html.j2`,
  `kanban.html.j2`, `comparison.html.j2`
- Current rendering: `pipeline/render_chart.py`, `main.py:1424-1481`

## Files touched

- `server/screenshot.py` — **new** — headless screenshot engine
- `server/render_route.py` — **new** — `/render/<type>` Chainlit route
- `server/md_postprocess.py` — **new** — markdown block → image replacer
- `renderers/` — **new** — plugin folder (mermaid, plotly, gantt, burndown, kanban, comparison, scatter, bar)
- `renderers/base.py` — **new** — BaseRenderer ABC
- `pipeline/render_chart.py` — keep builder functions, called by plugins
- `main.py` — register render route, wire post-processor before send
- `templates/` — **retired** — contents moved into `renderers/<type>/`
- `pyproject.toml` — add `playwright` dependency

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `GET /render/mermaid?diagram=graph+TD;A-->B` returns a PNG
- [ ] Same URL with `&mode=viewer` returns interactive HTML with live Mermaid
- [ ] Mermaid fenced code blocks in assistant output are auto-replaced with inline images + clickable links
- [ ] Plotly JSON output renders as screenshot with interactive link
- [ ] Existing chart types (gantt, burndown, kanban, comparison) work through the new plugin system with no regression
- [ ] New chart types (scatter, bar) render correctly
- [ ] Browser instance is pooled — no cold Chromium launch per screenshot
- [ ] Screenshots are cached by content hash — identical content returns cached PNG
- [ ] Adding a new chart type requires only creating a new folder under `renderers/` — no central registry changes
- [ ] `templates/` folder is retired, all `.html.j2` files live in their plugin folders

## Out of scope (defer)

- PDF export (future — same screenshot engine, different output format)
- Custom CSS themes for templates (v2)
- Remote rendering service (keep it local/in-process for now)

issues.title: [P4.24] renderers — template-based HTML→PNG screenshot service with plugin folder

issues.body:
**Scope**

Replace today's tool-call presentation in `server/foreman_agent.py:dispatch`
with a structured **plan → execute → reason** flow. Right now every tool
call lands as its own `_Using tool:_ name` + `json` chat message, the
final assistant reply is posted in a single non-streamed block after the
SDK finishes, and the model's thinking is never surfaced. The admin
sees *what* ran, never *why* it was chosen or what the model concluded
between calls.

Goals, in priority order:

1. **Show the plan before it runs.** When the model announces tool calls
   for the turn, render a checklist of intended tools *first* — not a
   ragged stream of \"Using tool:\" messages interleaved with output.
2. **Collapse per-tool chatter into a status checklist.** Each tool is a
   single line with a status: `⏳ waiting` → `✅ ok` / `❌ error`. No json
   arg dump and no bash output dump in the primary view — the full
   call + output lives inside the foldable step for the curious.
3. **Stream the post-tool reasoning.** After tools resolve, the model's
   follow-up prose should token-stream into the chat rather than block
   until the full turn finishes.
4. **Foldable live thinking.** Expose the SDK's thinking/reasoning blocks
   in a collapsed-by-default `cl.Step` that updates live, so the admin
   can pop it open mid-turn to watch the model reason in real time.

## Current behaviour (before)

In [server/foreman_agent.py:1399-1434](server/foreman_agent.py#L1399-L1434):

```python
async for message in client.receive_response():
    if isinstance(message, AssistantMessage):
        for block in message.content:
            if isinstance(block, TextBlock):
                assistant_chunks.append(block.text)        # buffered, not streamed
            elif isinstance(block, ToolUseBlock):
                await say(                                   # one chat message per tool
                    f\"_Using tool:_ \`{block.name}\`\n\"
                    f\"```json\n{args_preview}\n```\"
                )
# ...
reply = \"\".join(assistant_chunks).strip()
if reply:
    await say(reply)
```

Problems:
- Admin sees raw JSON args but not *why* the tool was picked.
- Each tool call is a new top-level chat message — vertical spam.
- Tool output is echoed into the `intercept.py` log step, then into the
  model's next assistant chunk, then the final reply — triple reading.
- Thinking blocks emitted by the SDK are discarded.

## Proposed flow (after)

**One** pinned assistant message per turn, composed of:

### Header — plan checklist

`Foreman's plan:` followed by an ordered checklist:

- [ ] `list_issues(state=\"open\", label=\"in-progress\")` — *to see what's actively being worked*
- [ ] `get_issue(number=42)` — *needs the latest comment thread before proposing next step*
- [ ] `run_render_chart(kind=\"burndown\")` — *to attach an updated burndown to the reply*

Built from the first `ToolUseBlock` batch before any tool executes. If
the model emits calls in waves (pre-tool plan + post-result follow-ups),
append new entries as they arrive rather than rewriting the whole list.

### Execution — status only

Each checkbox flips to `✅` / `❌` as the matching tool result lands.
Status text shows `· 1.2s` duration and `· rc=0` where applicable. No
other chat messages for tool noise.

### Foldable details per tool

Inside each checklist row, a `cl.Step(default_open=False, type=\"tool\")`:
- **Input:** the tool name + args (the JSON that today goes straight to chat).
- **Output:** what the tool returned, trimmed to ~2KB with a
  `… truncated, N bytes total` tail and the full payload attached as a
  `cl.File` for deep dives.

### Streamed reasoning

Assistant `TextBlock`s arriving after tool results stream in real time
(append via `cl.Message.stream_token()` or equivalent). The existing
buffer-and-post-at-end pattern is replaced with incremental updates.

### Foldable thinking step

A single top-of-turn `cl.Step(name=\"Foreman's thinking\", type=\"run\",
default_open=False)` that appends every thinking/reasoning block (if the
SDK exposes them — investigate `ClaudeSDKClient` response types beyond
`AssistantMessage` / `ResultMessage`) as a bullet with a wall-clock
timestamp. Collapsed by default; admin pops it open to watch live.

## Files touched

- [server/foreman_agent.py](server/foreman_agent.py) — rework the
  `receive_response` loop; introduce a `TurnRenderer` helper that owns
  the checklist, streaming message, and thinking step so `dispatch`
  stays readable.
- [main.py](main.py) — `_foreman_say` currently assumes one-shot
  `cl.Message` posts. Add a `_foreman_stream_start()` /
  `_foreman_stream_token()` / `_foreman_stream_end()` seam (or pass a
  callback object via `dispatch(...)`) so streaming works without
  `server/foreman_agent.py` importing Chainlit directly.
- Tests: unit coverage for the renderer with a fake `AssistantMessage`
  stream (plan → tool batch → tool results → text stream → another
  tool batch → final text).

## References

- KKallas/Imp#24 — Chainlit control surfaces (TaskList + abort). The
  checklist here is *per turn*; the TaskList there is *per running
  subprocess*. They don't overlap but should visually rhyme.
- KKallas/Imp#45 — Chat history. Streaming text must still end up in the
  persisted transcript.
- v0.1.md *The Agent's Role* — the system prompt this UI wraps.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A turn with N tool calls produces exactly **one** top-level chat
  message per assistant reply, containing the plan-checklist header +
  streamed reasoning. No `_Using tool:_` spam.
- [ ] Each checklist item flips from ⏳ → ✅/❌ in real time as the tool
  resolves; status includes duration and return code where applicable.
- [ ] Clicking a checklist item (or the associated `cl.Step`) reveals
  the full tool args and output; default state is collapsed.
- [ ] Post-tool assistant prose renders token-by-token (visibly
  streaming) rather than appearing as a finished block at turn end.
- [ ] A foldable `Foreman's thinking` step appears at the start of
  every turn, collapsed by default, updating live as the SDK emits
  thinking blocks. If the SDK version in use doesn't expose them yet,
  the step is created but empty — note the SDK version gap as a
  follow-up comment rather than blocking the rest of the work.
- [ ] No regression for turns with zero tool calls — they render as
  today: single streamed assistant message, no checklist.
- [ ] `tests/` covers: tool-batch-before-text, interleaved tool batches,
  tool failure, zero tools, thinking-block presence/absence.
- [ ] `server/foreman_agent.py` stays UI-framework-agnostic — it talks
  to `main.py` through the existing seam (`say`, `thinking`, `chart`)
  extended with streaming primitives; no direct `chainlit` imports in
  `server/`.

## Out of scope

- Changing *which* tools Foreman has access to.
- Persisting turn transcripts for replay (KKallas/Imp#45 covers that).
- Cancel-a-plan-mid-execution (KKallas/Imp#24 abort button covers that
  once the running-tasks map lands).
- Rewriting the `intercept.py` verdict / log-sidebar UX — only the SDK
  tool-call surface changes here.

issues.title: [P4.23] Foreman tool-calling UX — plan, checklist, streamed reasoning, foldable thinking

issues.body:
**Scope**

Foreman occasionally generates mermaid syntax in its chat replies
even though Chainlit can't render mermaid (and the system prompt
explicitly tells it not to). When that happens, the user sees raw
mermaid code instead of a chart. This issue adds a **watchdog in
`main.py._foreman_say`**: scan every outbound Foreman reply for
fenced mermaid blocks, convert supported ones (gantt for now) to
inline `cl.Plotly` elements, and strip them from the text.

Net effect: the system prompt is no longer the only line of defence
— even when Foreman ignores the instruction, the watchdog catches
the mermaid before it lands in chat.

## Watchdog flow

```python
async def _foreman_say(text: str) -> None:
    blocks = _extract_mermaid_blocks(text)
    plotly_elements: list[cl.Plotly] = []
    cleaned_text = text

    for block in blocks:
        if block["lang"] == "mermaid" and block["content"].lstrip().startswith("gantt"):
            try:
                figure = _mermaid_gantt_to_plotly(block["content"])
                plotly_elements.append(
                    cl.Plotly(name="auto-gantt", figure=figure, display="inline")
                )
                cleaned_text = cleaned_text.replace(block["raw"], "")
            except Exception as exc:
                # Keep the original block but add a note
                cleaned_text = cleaned_text.replace(
                    block["raw"],
                    block["raw"] + f"\n\n_(watchdog couldn't parse this gantt: {exc})_",
                )
        # Other mermaid types (flowchart, sequence, etc.) — leave in place
        # with a note "mermaid <type> isn't rendered inline yet"

    await cl.Message(
        author="Foreman",
        content=cleaned_text.strip(),
        elements=plotly_elements,
    ).send()
```

## Mermaid gantt parser

`pipeline/mermaid_to_plotly.py` (new):

- Line-based parser, no external dependencies (mermaid grammar is
  small enough to handle directly)
- Recognises:
  - `gantt`
  - `title <text>`
  - `dateFormat <format>`
  - `axisFormat <format>`
  - `excludes weekends` / `excludes <date>`
  - `section <name>`
  - Task lines: `Task name :id, start, duration` or
    `Task name :tag, id, start, duration` or
    `Task name :tag, id, after dep, duration` (chained `after`)
- Returns the same structure `render_chart.build_mermaid_gantt`
  produces, so we can reuse `build_plotly_figure` directly.

## Foreman system prompt update

The watchdog is belt-and-suspenders, not a license to spew mermaid.
Update the prompt to be even more explicit:

> "Mermaid code blocks WILL NOT RENDER in chat under any
> circumstances. If you produce one anyway, an automated watchdog
> will try to convert it to a Plotly chart and may fail. Use
> `run_render_chart` for canonical charts; for one-off / custom
> data, write a `python -c` script that builds a Plotly figure
> dict to `.imp/output/<name>.plotly.json` and call
> `render_plotly_file(<path>)` (added by KKallas/Imp#TBD if we
> open it). Don't paste mermaid into your prose."

## Other mermaid types

For mermaid blocks that aren't `gantt`:

- `flowchart` / `graph` — leave as-is with a "_(flowchart rendering
  isn't supported inline yet)_" note. Could add later if we find a
  Plotly equivalent (Sankey? Network graph?)
- `sequence`, `pie`, `state`, etc. — same: leave + note

## References

- KKallas/Imp#14 — render_chart (we reuse `build_plotly_figure`)
- KKallas/Imp#46 — Guard code review (orthogonal — this is about
  rendering, not security)
- v0.1.md — the original "emit mermaid blocks" guidance (now
  contradicted by reality; doc update is part of this issue)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `pipeline/mermaid_to_plotly.py` exposes
  `parse_gantt(text) -> {tasks, sections, title, ...}` and
  `to_plotly_figure(parsed) -> dict` (or a single
  `mermaid_gantt_to_plotly(text) -> figure`)
- [ ] Watchdog in `main.py._foreman_say` extracts mermaid blocks,
  converts gantt ones, attaches as `cl.Plotly`, strips from text
- [ ] Non-gantt mermaid blocks left in place with a "not rendered
  inline" note (so the user at least knows the agent meant to draw
  something)
- [ ] Watchdog fails soft: parse error → leave the block + note
  the failure, don't break the whole reply
- [ ] Foreman system prompt updated with the stronger anti-mermaid
  language
- [ ] v0.1.md updated to reflect reality (no more "emit mermaid
  blocks" guidance for charts)
- [ ] Tests:
  - `_extract_mermaid_blocks` finds all fenced blocks, handles
    nested code fences, handles missing closing fence
  - `parse_gantt` round-trips a simple gantt + a complex one (the
    one from the user's transcript: weekend exclusions, sections,
    dependencies, crit tags)
  - `to_plotly_figure` produces the expected bar shapes
  - watchdog integration: an agent reply with a mermaid gantt block
    produces a `cl.Plotly` element + stripped text
  - watchdog fail-soft: a malformed mermaid gantt → original text
    preserved + error note appended

## Out of scope

- Round-tripping non-gantt mermaid types (could be follow-up issues
  per type if anyone wants it)
- Server-side mermaid rendering (e.g. shipping the mermaid CLI and
  rendering to PNG) — Plotly is more interactive and we already
  have the infrastructure
- Editing the agent's reply mid-stream (token-by-token replacement
  of mermaid as it arrives). Static post-processing is enough.

issues.body:
**Scope**

Two changes:

1. **Restructure tools into self-contained folders** — same pattern as
   \`renderers/\`. Each tool gets its own directory with a README,
   the script, and any supporting files. This replaces the flat
   \`99-tools/\` and \`pipeline/\` layouts.
2. **Build a heuristics tool** that generates best estimates for dates
   and durations of project parts, calibrated from the project's own
   closed-issue history.

## Tool folder structure

Mirror the \`renderers/<type>/\` pattern: each tool is a self-contained
folder under \`tools/\`. The folder name is the tool's identity.

\`\`\`
tools/
├── __init__.py              # tool discovery (scan subdirs)
│
├── heuristics/              # duration estimation & date inference
│   ├── README.md            # what it does, inputs, outputs, examples
│   ├── calibrate.py         # S/M/L duration calibration from closed issues
│   ├── infer.py             # per-issue date/duration inference
│   └── tests/
│       └── test_calibrate.py
│
├── sync_issues/             # GitHub issue sync
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── sync.py              # migrated from pipeline/sync_issues.py
│   └── ...
│
├── render_chart/            # chart rendering (calls renderers/)
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── render.py            # migrated from pipeline/render_chart.py
│   └── ...
│
├── moderate_issues/         # issue moderation
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── moderate.py          # migrated from 99-tools/moderate_issues.py
│   └── moderate_issues.md   # prompt/instructions
│
├── fix_prs/                 # PR fixing
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── fix.py               # migrated from 99-tools/fix_prs.py
│   ├── fix_prs_analysis.md
│   └── fix_prs_fix.md
│
├── solve_issues/            # issue solving
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── solve.py             # migrated from 99-tools/solve_issues.py
│   └── solve_issues.md
│
├── estimate_dates/          # date estimation
│   ├── README.md
│   └── estimate.py          # migrated from pipeline/estimate_dates.py
│
├── scenarios/               # what-if scenario engine
│   ├── README.md
│   └── scenarios.py         # migrated from pipeline/scenarios.py
│
├── project_bootstrap/       # project setup & field provisioning
│   ├── README.md
│   └── bootstrap.py         # migrated from pipeline/project_bootstrap.py
│
└── mermaid_to_plotly/       # mermaid → plotly conversion
    ├── README.md
    └── convert.py           # migrated from pipeline/mermaid_to_plotly.py
\`\`\`

Each README follows a standard template:
\`\`\`markdown
# Tool Name

One-line description.

## Inputs
- What data/files it reads

## Outputs
- What it produces

## Usage
\\\`\\\`\\\`bash
python -m tools.heuristics.calibrate
\\\`\\\`\\\`

## Foreman integration
How Foreman calls this tool (MCP tool name, args).
\`\`\`

## Heuristics tool — duration calibration

Replace the per-label duration hardcodes in the current
\`pipeline/heuristics.py\` with **project-calibrated** S/M/L buckets
derived from the project's own closed-issue history.

### Algorithm

1. \`tools/heuristics/calibrate.py\` exposes
   \`calibrate(closed_issues, defaults=(1, 2, 3)) -> dict[str, int]\`
2. For each closed issue, compute \`delta_days = closedAt - createdAt\`
3. Bucket by **complexity signal**:
   - \`large\`: > 5 acceptance-criteria checkboxes or > 1500 chars body
   - \`medium\`: 2–5 ACs or 500–1500 chars
   - \`small\`: otherwise
4. Per bucket, take the **median** delta_days (robust to outliers)
5. Empty bucket → fall back to default
6. Cache to \`.imp/calibration.json\` with \`calibrated_at\` timestamp;
   recompute when stale (>24h) or closed-issue count changed

### Inference

\`tools/heuristics/infer.py\` replaces the current
\`heuristics.infer_duration\`:

\`\`\`python
def infer_duration(issue, calibration):
    raw = field_value(issue, "duration_days")
    if raw and raw > 0:
        return (int(raw), "github", "high")

    bucket = complexity_bucket(issue)  # small | medium | large
    return (calibration[bucket], "heuristic", "medium")
\`\`\`

### Foreman integration

MCP tool \`get_duration_estimates()\` returns the current calibration
dict + when it was computed + sample sizes per bucket. Foreman calls
this when asked "how long should this take?" without running the full
pipeline.

## Migration plan

The tool folder restructure is a **rename + reorganize**, not a
rewrite. Existing code moves into its folder with minimal changes.
Import paths update in \`server/foreman_agent.py\` and \`main.py\`.

Phase 1: Create \`tools/heuristics/\` with the new calibration code
Phase 2: Migrate remaining tools from \`99-tools/\` and \`pipeline/\`
         into \`tools/<name>/\` folders (can be a follow-up PR)

## References

- #60 — P4.24 renderers (same folder-per-plugin pattern)
- #13 — original heuristics (P4.13)
- #14 — render_chart (consumes calibrated durations)
- #11 — Foreman (gets the MCP tool)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] \`tools/heuristics/calibrate.py\` exposes \`calibrate()\` with
      defaults + complexity-bucket logic
- [ ] Median used per bucket (not mean)
- [ ] Result cached to \`.imp/calibration.json\`; recomputed when stale
- [ ] \`tools/heuristics/infer.py\` uses calibration dict instead of
      static label table
- [ ] \`complexity_bucket(issue)\` shared between calibrator and inferrer
- [ ] Foreman MCP tool \`get_duration_estimates()\` returns
      \`{small, medium, large, calibrated_at, sample_sizes}\`
- [ ] Each tool folder has a README with standard template
- [ ] Tests for calibrate: empty input → defaults, mixed issues → medians,
      cache hit/miss, heuristics integration

## Out of scope

- Per-area-label calibration (later if S/M/L proves too coarse)
- Migrating ALL tools in this PR (heuristics first, others follow)
- Persistent learning across installs (needs v0.2 central server)

issues.title: [P4.21] heuristics — calibrate small/medium/large durations from closed issues

issues.body:
**Scope**

Once v0.2.2 is in place — every contribution signed with a verifiable
`(who, when, what, parent)` tuple in an append-only transparency log
— the central registry has the data needed to keep the commons clean
at scale. This issue ships the moderation / governance layer that
makes the commons trustworthy enough to actually use.

Three workflows:

1. **Automated copy-detection agent** — scans the corpus for
   suspiciously similar tools without legitimate lineage chains;
   flags candidates for human review.
2. **Takedown process** — IP holders can flag infringing tools;
   removed from the served corpus but retained in the transparency
   log (with a takedown receipt) so history stays verifiable.
3. **Deprecation + reputation** — contributors and tools accumulate
   signal over time; deprecated tools are filtered out of search
   results by default but remain accessible by explicit lookup.

## Automated copy-detection agent

Periodically (cron-style on the central server, or triggered by
contribution rate):

```
1. For each tool T in the corpus:
   a. Find all tools T' within cosine distance < 0.05 (essentially
      identical embeddings)
   b. For each (T, T') pair:
      i. Check timestamps: which came first?
      ii. Check parent chains: is T' legitimately derived from T's
          lineage (via parent_tool_id chain)?
      iii. If unrelated lineages but extreme similarity → flag for
           human review (potential plagiarism or inadvertent
           duplication)
2. Flagged pairs go to a moderator queue; human reviewer decides:
   - Legitimate (different solutions to same problem) → no action
   - Inadvertent duplicate (contributor didn't search first) → keep
     earlier, deprecate later, notify the later contributor
   - Plagiarism → takedown the later tool, flag the contributor
```

The cosine threshold + lineage check is the key insight: similarity
alone isn't suspicious; similarity WITHOUT lineage is.

## Takedown process

- Public takedown form: `{infringing_tool_id, claimant_identity,
  evidence, signed_declaration}`
- Reviewer (initially the registry operator; later a governance
  committee) decides within an SLA (e.g. 7 days)
- On takedown: tool removed from the served corpus, but the
  transparency log entry stays — replaced by a "takedown receipt"
  that records `(original_entry_id, reason, decision_date,
  reviewer)`. Anyone can audit that the takedown happened, even
  though the content itself is gone.
- Counter-notice process for the original contributor (DMCA-style)
- Repeat-offender contributor accounts → suspension

## Deprecation workflow

- Contributors can mark their own tools deprecated (with a reason
  + optional pointer to a successor tool)
- Tools auto-deprecated when:
  - A successor tool with the same name and a parent_tool link to
    this one has higher reputation
  - The dependency tools have all been deprecated
- Deprecated tools:
  - Filtered out of `find_similar_tool` results by default
  - Still returned with `include_deprecated=True`
  - Carry a banner in the metadata so the agent can warn

## Reputation

Lightweight signal, NOT karma-style scoring (which gets gamed):

**Per-tool**:
- Number of times retrieved (proxy for usefulness)
- Number of variants forked from it (proxy for inspiration)
- Active deprecation status (binary)

**Per-owner-account** (the legal entity, not per-agent):
- Total contributions accepted
- Number of takedowns against contributions (negative signal)
- Number of confirmed plagiarism findings (strong negative signal)

Reputation surfaces in search results as a tiebreaker, not a primary
sort key. Low-rep contributors aren't excluded; their tools just
appear later when there are equivalent alternatives.

## Governance

Initial setup:

- Single-operator phase: the registry operator (Imp Inc. / foundation
  TBD) makes all moderation decisions
- Governance docs published from day one: rules of the road, takedown
  process, appeals
- Community input via a public discussion forum (issues on the
  registry's repo)

Long-term (when contribution rate justifies):

- Multi-reviewer moderator pool
- Elected steering committee from active contributors
- Charter / bylaws

This issue ships the **infrastructure** for governance (data, tools,
processes); the **decisions** about who governs and how are a
separate non-technical workstream.

## References

- v0.2.1 — Local foundation
- v0.2.2 — Central registry + signatures (this issue depends on the
  signature + transparency log infrastructure)
- KKallas/Imp#46 — Guard code review (independent gate at execution
  time; doesn't replace this layer)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Copy-detection agent: scans corpus on schedule, flags
  high-similarity / no-lineage pairs to a moderator queue
- [ ] Moderator queue UI (web interface, doesn't have to be pretty —
  internal tool) for human review of flagged pairs
- [ ] Takedown form (public) + processing workflow with SLA
- [ ] Takedown receipts in the transparency log replace the original
  entry but preserve auditability
- [ ] Counter-notice process for contributors
- [ ] Repeat-offender suspension policy enforced at the owner-account
  level (not per-agent)
- [ ] Deprecation: contributor self-service + automatic per the rules
  above
- [ ] Search filters deprecated tools by default; explicit
  `include_deprecated=True` returns them
- [ ] Reputation signals (per-tool + per-owner-account) computed and
  surfaced as search tiebreakers
- [ ] Governance docs published: rules, takedown process, appeals,
  contributor agreement
- [ ] Tests:
  - Copy-detection agent fires on a planted similar-without-lineage
    pair, doesn't fire on legitimate-lineage variants
  - Takedown removes from search but preserves log entry
  - Counter-notice round-trip
  - Deprecation banner surfaces in search results
  - Reputation tiebreakers behave deterministically given inputs

## Out of scope

- Bulk-import of tools from external sources (PyPI, GitHub) — could
  seed the corpus, but adds a different IP risk surface; separate
  decision
- Automated patent search (impractical and likely counterproductive)
- Karma / leaderboard / contributor-of-the-month (gameable, distracts
  from the actual goal of useful tools)
- Multi-language tool support (Python only for v0.2)

issues.title: [v0.2.3] Quality & governance — automated copy-detection, takedown, deprecation, reputation

issues.body:
**Scope**

The networked half of the Tool Repository. Adds a central server that
acts as Certificate Authority (CA) for per-agent identities, hosts the
canonical tool corpus, and issues short-lived certs to registered
agent installs. Every upload and download is signed; provenance
remains verifiable even if the central DB is one day taken offline.

Builds on v0.2.1 (local-only foundation). When this lands, the local
store keeps **only vectors + minimal metadata**; the actual tool code
lives on the central server and is fetched on-demand after a vector
match.

## Identity model

Three layers, top-down:

1. **Central Root CA** — owned by the registry operator. Long-lived
   key. Public root cert published to multiple immutable mirrors
   (DNSSEC TXT, IPFS, gist mirror, etc.) so verifiers can chain
   back to it after any future DB demise.

2. **Owner Account** — registered entity. Required attributes:
   - At LEAST ONE of `{human_owner, company}`; can have BOTH.
   - Verified email (for human) or domain ownership (for company).
   - Active payment method (registration is paid).
   - Signed ToS + contribution agreement.

3. **Agent Cert** — ephemeral, per Imp install. Issued by the central
   CA on behalf of the owner. Sigstore-style keyless: short-lived
   (hours to days), refreshed automatically, no long-lived secret on
   the agent's host. Compromise of one agent's key → blast radius is
   that agent's contributions during the cert's lifetime; not a
   catastrophe.

```
Central Root CA
  └── Owner Account  (human and/or company; ToS-bound)
        └── Agent Cert  (ephemeral, per Imp install)
```

## Signature payload

Every contribution is signed over a tuple:

```
(content_hash, contributor_agent_id, owner_account_id, timestamp, parent_tool_id?)
```

- `content_hash` — sha256 of the tool file (frontmatter + code)
- `contributor_agent_id` — the agent's cert subject
- `owner_account_id` — the account the agent rolls up to
- `timestamp` — server-issued, NOT client-supplied (defeats backdating)
- `parent_tool_id` — lineage link (when this tool was derived from
  an existing one; nullable for net-new contributions)

The bundle of `(content_hash + signature + timestamp + lineage)` IS
what gets stored in the transparency log — content and signature
together, not the code alone.

## Transparency log

Append-only public log, Certificate-Transparency-style:

- Every contribution entry hashed into a Merkle tree
- Root published to multiple mirrors at regular intervals
- Anyone can audit: download the log, verify the chain, re-derive
  the root, confirm against the published mirror
- Defeats silent retraction by the registry operator (you can prove
  what was logged at time T, even if the operator later removes it
  from the served corpus)

Keeps the central operator honest. Registry operator can still refuse
to accept a contribution OR remove a contribution from the served
corpus, but they CAN'T silently rewrite history about what was
contributed when by whom.

## Registration flow

```
1. Admin runs `imp register` (or via Setup Agent)
2. Imp prompts for owner type:
   - Human: email + payment + accept ToS
   - Company: domain + DNS verification + payment + accept ToS
   - Both: do both flows; account links them
3. Central server creates Owner Account
4. Imp install requests an Agent Cert (Sigstore-style flow, short-lived)
5. Cert renewed automatically while subscription is active
```

If subscription lapses → no new agent certs issued → existing certs
expire → install reverts to local-only mode (degraded but functional).

## Search / contribute over the network

**Search:**

```
1. Agent embeds query → query vector
2. Local index lookup → top-k vector matches (only vectors stored locally)
3. For each match within distance threshold → fetch full code from central
4. Verify the central's signature on the returned code
5. Verify the contributor's signature on the original contribution
6. Run Guard's code review checklist (KKallas/Imp#46) before execution
```

**Contribute:**

```
1. Agent has new / edited tool to contribute
2. Sanitization layer (from v0.2.1) runs; admin previews + approves
3. Agent signs (content_hash, agent_id, owner_id, parent_id?) with its
   ephemeral cert
4. Upload to central → central server signs the timestamp + appends to
   the transparency log → returns the signed receipt
5. Local index updated with the new vector
```

## ToS / contribution agreement

Required at registration time:

- **Original-work representation**: contributor warrants the tool is
  their original work or properly licensed for redistribution under
  the registry's chosen license (Apache-2.0 or MIT — pick one).
- **License contamination check**: contributor warrants the tool
  doesn't carry GPL/AGPL/proprietary headers (registry runs a regex
  scan as belt-and-suspenders; flagged tools quarantined).
- **Indemnification**: contributor indemnifies the registry against
  IP claims arising from their contribution.
- **Reuse license**: contributor grants downstream consumers the
  registry's chosen license terms.

Standard OSS commons language. Gets reviewed by an actual lawyer
before the registry is publicly launched.

## Self-hosted mode (v0.1 compatibility)

Imp installs that don't register stay on **local-only mode** (the
v0.2.1 path). They use their own `.imp/tool_repo/` exclusively, no
network calls, no signed contributions. Federation between
self-hosted and public is explicitly out of scope; that's a
deliberate architectural simplification.

## References

- v0.2.1 — Local foundation (this issue depends on it)
- v0.2.3 — Quality & governance (depends on this issue's signature
  infrastructure)
- KKallas/Imp#46 — Guard code review (gates execution of pulled tools)
- Sigstore (sigstore.dev) — adopt the OSS primitives, don't roll
  custom crypto
- Certificate Transparency RFC 6962 — pattern for the append-only log

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Central server (separate codebase / repo TBD) accepts
  registration, issues agent certs, accepts signed contributions,
  serves signed downloads, maintains the transparency log
- [ ] Agent client supports: registration handshake, cert refresh,
  signed upload, verified download, Guard review of fetched code
- [ ] Local store contains ONLY vectors + name + description + args
  (NOT code) — code fetched on match
- [ ] Owner account requires at least one of `{human, company}`;
  supports both
- [ ] Agent certs are short-lived (hours, not months); auto-refresh
  while owner subscription is active
- [ ] Every contribution carries a signature over `(content_hash,
  agent_id, owner_id, server_timestamp, parent_id?)`
- [ ] Transparency log: append-only, Merkle-rooted, root published
  to ≥ 3 independent mirrors at a regular cadence
- [ ] License contamination scan at intake (regex for GPL / AGPL /
  proprietary headers); contaminated → quarantined, not auto-accepted
- [ ] ToS infrastructure: registration captures explicit acceptance,
  audit log retains the signed acceptance per owner
- [ ] Subscription lapse degrades gracefully to local-only mode
- [ ] Tests:
  - Round-trip: register → contribute → search → fetch → verify
    signatures
  - Backdated client timestamp rejected (server timestamp wins)
  - Tampered tool body fails verification
  - Expired agent cert refused at upload AND download
  - Transparency log entries verifiable against the published root
  - License-contaminated tool quarantined at intake

## Out of scope (defer to v0.2.3)

- Automated copy-detection / similarity scans across the corpus
- DMCA-style takedown process
- Contributor reputation / quality scoring
- Deprecation workflow for old tools

## Out of scope (later)

- Federation between self-hosted and public registries
- Multiple regional / specialised registries (one canonical for v0.2)
- Web UI for browsing the public corpus

issues.title: [v0.2.2] Central registry — Sigstore-style per-agent identity, signed contributions, transparency log

issues.body:
**Scope**

Foundation for the v0.2 Central Tool Server: a local-only, vector-indexed
repository of reusable Python tools that the agent can search before
writing new code from scratch and contribute to when its edits diverge
from the retrieved base.

**No network, no signing yet** — that lands in v0.2.2. This issue
proves the core flow inside a single Imp install:

  agent needs X → embed query → search local index → top-k matches
  → agent edits the closest one → if diff ≥ threshold → contribute
  back to the local index for next time.

## Tool spec

Each tool is a Python file with structured frontmatter:

```python
# ---imp-tool---
# name: compute_field_aggregate
# description: Aggregate a field across filtered issues.
# args: {field: str, agg: Literal["sum","mean","min","max","count"], filter: dict}
# parent: null
# version: 1
# created_at: 2026-04-15T12:34:56Z
# ---end---
"""Aggregate a numeric field across the filtered subset of issues.

Args:
    field: name of the field on each issue to aggregate.
    agg:   sum / mean / min / max / count.
    filter: dict mapping field name → required value.

Returns:
    The aggregate value (int / float / count).
"""
def compute_field_aggregate(field, agg, filter):
    ...
```

Stored under `.imp/tool_repo/tools/<name>__<short_hash>.py`. The
`<short_hash>` lets multiple variants of the same name coexist.

## Vector index

Stored as **sqlite + sqlite-vec** in `.imp/tool_repo/index.db`.
Zero external services, single-file, easy to inspect.

What gets embedded (concatenated, then sent to the embedding model):

```
NAME: <name>
DESCRIPTION: <description from frontmatter>
ARGS: <args schema as JSON>
DOCSTRING: <module / function docstring>
CODE_SUMMARY: <normalized code: comments stripped, identifiers preserved>
```

Each section weighted distinctly when computing the final vector — the
docstring and description are highest signal for "what does it do",
the code summary catches "different implementations of the same idea."

Local store keeps **vectors + name + description + args**, NOT the
full code. (For v0.2.1, with no network, "full code" is on disk
anyway in the same `.imp/tool_repo/tools/` dir; this constraint
matters more once v0.2.2 makes the actual code live on a remote
server.)

## Embedding backend

Abstraction over an embedding API. At least one implementation —
configurable via `.imp/config.json`:

```json
"tool_repo": {
  "embedding_backend": "anthropic" | "openai" | "local-st",
  "embedding_model": "...",
  "contribution_threshold": 0.20
}
```

Tests use a deterministic fake backend (hashes the input → vector)
so they don't depend on a live API.

## MCP tools (registered with Foreman)

- `find_similar_tool(query: str, k: int = 3) -> list[{name, description, distance, path}]`
  — embed `query`, return top-k matches with cosine distances. Distance
  threshold for "useful match" is configurable.

- `contribute_tool(name: str, code: str, parent_tool: str | None = None) -> {path, vector_id}`
  — write the tool to disk, embed it, insert into the index. Returns
  the path (so the agent can show the admin what got added).

## Diff metric

For deciding "is this edit ≥ 20% different from the parent":

**MVP: cosine distance on the embeddings.** Same vector infrastructure,
no extra dependency, intuitive ("how semantically different is the new
tool from its parent?"). Threshold is configurable. If calibration
proves bad, we can add AST-canonicalised Levenshtein in v0.2.2 as an
alternative.

## Sanitization layer (admin preview)

Before any tool is contributed (even locally), it goes through:

1. **Heuristic strip pass** — remove proper-noun-y comments, strip
   identifier prefixes that look company-specific (configurable
   wordlist of "things to scrub": company name, customer names,
   internal team names from `.imp/config.json`).
2. **Admin preview** — render a diff (proposed tool ↓, what would
   be contributed ↑) and ask the admin to approve via an action
   button on the chat. (Same `cl.Action` pattern as the budget bar.)
3. **Audit log** — every contribution is logged in
   `.imp/tool_repo/audit.jsonl` with timestamp, source request,
   parent tool (if any), and the admin's decision.

For v0.2.1 (local-only), this is "good practice." For v0.2.2 (network),
it becomes essential.

## Foreman integration

System prompt addendum: "Before writing a new tool from scratch, call
`find_similar_tool` with a description of what you need. If a match
within distance < 0.3 exists, use it (with light edits if needed). If
no good match, write the tool fresh and call `contribute_tool` so the
next agent can find it."

## References

- KKallas/Imp#11 — Foreman, the consumer of tool MCP calls
- KKallas/Imp#46 — Guard code review (still gates execution; this
  issue is about retrieval / storage, not trust)
- v0.2.2 — Central registry: signed network protocol (depends on
  this issue's foundation)
- v0.2.3 — Quality & governance: copy-detection, takedown

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `.imp/tool_repo/` directory layout: `tools/`, `index.db`, `audit.jsonl`
- [ ] Tool spec frontmatter parser + serializer
- [ ] sqlite-vec (or equivalent) index in `index.db`
- [ ] Configurable embedding backend with at least one implementation
  (and a deterministic fake for tests)
- [ ] MCP tools `find_similar_tool` + `contribute_tool` registered
  with Foreman
- [ ] Diff metric (cosine distance on embeddings) computes "% different"
  from parent; configurable threshold (default 20%)
- [ ] Sanitization layer: heuristic strip pass + admin preview before
  contribute (chat action button to approve / reject / edit)
- [ ] Audit log of every contribution decision
- [ ] Foreman system prompt addendum guides "search before writing"
- [ ] Tests:
  - Tool spec round-trip (frontmatter parse + emit)
  - Index insert + similarity search + threshold filtering
  - Diff metric correctness on known parent/variant pairs
  - Sanitization strips configured wordlist
  - Admin preview action callback persists the decision
  - Foreman tool wrappers exercised via fake intercept (same pattern
    as `tests/test_foreman_agent.py`)

## Out of scope (defer to v0.2.2 or later)

- Network sync (central server, registration, signed contributions)
- Cryptographic provenance (signatures, certs, transparency log)
- Public commons / multi-tenant
- Cross-language tool support
- Web UI for browsing the local repo (CLI / chat is enough for v0.2.1)
